subject: Sociology
question_text: Gender identity is not only formed in early childhood during primary socialisation but also shaped by the peer group, school and work place. (a) What is meant by â€˜identityâ€™? [2] (b) Describe two ways in which gender identity can be reinforced. [4] (c) Explain how the peer group contributes to secondary socialisation. [6] (d) Explain why inadequate socialisation can be a problem for society. [8] (e) To what extent is human behaviour learned through the processes of socialisation? [15] Section C: Social inequality
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) What is meant by â€˜identityâ€™? How individuals see and define themselves and how other people see and define them. One mark for partial definition, e.g. how others see us. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. how we see ourselves and how other people see and define us. (b) Describe two ways in which gender identity can be reinforced. [4] Candidates will be expected to identify and describe two from the following: â€¢ Canalisation â€¢ Manipulation â€¢ Coercion â€¢ Sanctions â€¢ Rewards â€¢ Secondary socialisation e.g. peer pressure â€¢ Primary socialisation â€¢ Verbal appellation â€¢ Role modelling in the media â€¢ Hidden curriculum â€¢ Other reasonable responses. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). (c) Explain how the peer group contributes to secondary socialisation. [6] Candidates need to demonstrate an understanding of the process of secondary socialisation in the peer group irrespective of age. Possible answers: â€¢ Anti-school sub-culture â€¢ Youth sub-culture â€¢ Need to fit in/conform/acceptance/ridicule/shunning/ostracism â€¢ Peer pressure â€¢ Examples of adult peer group influences, e.g. work place â€¢ Any other reasonable response. Page 9 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0495 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 Band 0 No creditworthy response. Band 1 (1â€“3) Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of the role of the peer groups in secondary socialisation. Responses may be short and un/under developed. At the top of the band, there may be a tendency towards simplistic answers, engaging with sociological ideas without using sociological language. Alternatively, candidates may offer a wider range of weak points. Answers may be placed broadly within the concept of secondary socialisation in general. Band 2 (4â€“6) A clear and accurate explanation showing good sociological knowledge and understanding of the role of the peer group in secondary socialisation, which is supported by relevant examples. Sociological terms should be expected. At the top of the band candidates will use sociological terms and concepts, referring to a range of factors. Candidates will address the contribution of the peer group in secondary socialisation. (d) Explain why inadequate socialisation can be a problem for society. [8] Candidates should show awareness of the argument that inadequate socialisation can threaten the stability of society. Possible answers: â€¢ Dysfunctional for society â€¢ Anomie â€¢ Crime and deviance â€¢ Encourages counter culture â€¢ Feral children â€¢ Encourages other social division, e.g. racism, homophobia, sexism â€¢ Undermines value consensus â€¢ Other reasonable responses. subject: Sociology
question_text: Gender is one form of social stratification where a personâ€™s status is ascribed not achieved. Those who do not have high status or power in society may suffer from discrimination and poor life chances. Some feminists claim that patriarchy exists in most societies and this leads to discrimination against women. (a) What is meant by â€˜achieved statusâ€™? [2] (b) Describe two forms of social stratification other than gender. [4] (c) Explain how people born with higher ascribed status can have better life chances than other social groups. [6] (d) Explain why lack of power may lead to discrimination. [8] (e) To what extent is patriarchy still a major feature of modern industrial society? mark_scheme_text: 3 (a) What is meant by â€˜achieved statusâ€™? [2] Position which is earned through a personâ€™s own efforts rather than inherited. One mark for partial definition, e.g . 'the status you get from working hard.' Two marks for clear definition, e.g. ' the status you get through your own efforts rather than status you inherit from your family.' (b) Describe two forms of social stratification other than gender. [4] Candidates will be expected to identify and describe two from the following: â€¢ Age â€¢ Class â€¢ Wealth/income â€¢ Ethnicity/race â€¢ Religion/ theocracy â€¢ Status â€¢ Power â€¢ Disability â€¢ Caste â€¢ Other reasonable responses. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). Page 13 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0495 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (c) Explain how people born with higher ascribed status can have better life chances than other social groups. [6] Candidates need to demonstrate an understanding of both what ascribed status means and life chances. Candidates who confuse ascribed and achieved status are unlikely to score highly. Possible answers: â€¢ Better access to education including top universities â€¢ â€˜old boysâ€™ network better access to higher status jobs â€¢ Better access to health care/more advanced health care â€¢ Access to political elite/ part of political classes â€¢ Access to technology/ media/knowledge â€¢ Better access to legal representation â€¢ Any other reasonable response. Band 0 No creditworthy response. Band 1 (1â€“3) Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of the concept of ascribed status and life chances. Responses may be short and un/under developed. At the top of the band, there may be a tendency towards simplistic answers, engaging with sociological ideas without using sociological language. Alternatively, candidates may offer a wider range of weak points. Answers may be placed broadly within the concept of life chances in general. Band 2 (4â€“6) A clear and accurate explanation showing good sociological knowledge and understanding of the concepts of ascribed status and life chances which is supported by relevant examples. Sociological terms should be expected. At the top of the band candidates will use sociological terms and concepts, referring to a range of factors. Candidates will address how life chances are improved relative to other social groups. (d) Explain why lack of power may lead to discrimination. [8] Candidates should show an understanding of the concepts of power and discrimination. Power need not be political power it can also be economic power in capitalist society. The question is clearly not intended to be power as in electricity, as this is not a sociological concept, although a candidate who argued that lack of electricity resulted in poor life chances/discrimination would deserve some credit. Possible answers: â€¢ Media stereotypes â€¢ Police labelling â€¢ Lack of rights â€¢ Patr

The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 0495/13/M/J/15 Section A: Theory and methods Source A India Provisional Population Totals Population Persons 1,210,193,422 Males 623,724,248 Females 586,469,174 Population growth 2001â€“2011 Absolute Percentage Persons 181,455,986 17.
mark_scheme_text: 1 (a) From the evidence in Source A, identify the gender with the lowest literacy rate and the gender with the highest percentage population growth [2] Lowest literacy: females (65.46) Highest population growth: female (18.12) One mark for each correctly identified. (Up to a maximum of two). Page 3 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0495 13 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (b) Identify two methods that could be used to collect quantitative data. [2] Any two from: â€¢ Postal questionnaires/questionnaire â€¢ Social survey â€¢ Telephone questionnaire â€¢ Interview with closed questions/structured â€¢ Census â€¢ Official statistics â€¢ Any other reasonable response NB do not allow just interview. Award one mark only for questionnaire, award two marks for two types of questionnaires. One mark for each correct method of selection identified (up to a maximum of two). (c) Using information from Source A, describe two reasons why quantitative data may not be valid. [4] Candidates need to demonstrate an understanding that the size of the task creates logistical issues of collecting and collating returns which might impact on validity. This is in addition to all the issues with the methods used. Candidates might also, taking their cue from the source, identify poor literacy rates as a potential problem for the validity too. As, due to low literacy rates, the census of India is carried out by officials asking householders questions which householders are legally obliged to answer truthfully. Reasons include: â€¢ Misinterpretation of question due to poor literacy rates or low levels of education â€¢ Methodology used/training of researchers/researcher bias â€¢ Respondents may be fearful of the official asking the question â€¢ Time required to collate the information may mean it is inaccurate before it is finished â€¢ Source material collated in 2011, therefore out of date â€¢ Internal migration/homeless may mean some sections of the population are not identified in the data â€¢ Errors in collating data â€¢ Adapted source/manipulation of data â€¢ Other reasonable response. Band 0 No creditworthy response. Band 1 (1â€“2) Answers at the bottom of the band will offer one reason without use of Source A. Alternatively; answers may be vague or general with little sociological knowledge. Answers at the top end of the mark band may offer two reasons but without use to Source A. subject: Sociology
question_text: The world has many diverse cultures but within a culture there may be sub-cultures. These sub- cultures often have hidden rules which are not apparent to the outsider. Members of the sub- culture must conform to the groupâ€™s rules, norms and values or they may face coercion. (a) What is meant by the term â€˜coercionâ€™? [2] (b) Describe two ways in which culture can be transmitted from one generation to another. [4] (c) Explain how coercion can be used to ensure social conformity. [6] (d) Explain why sub-cultures are formed. [8] (e) To what extent is the existence of sub-cultures evidence of non-conformity in society? [15] Section C: Social inequality
mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) What is meant by the term â€˜coercionâ€™? [2] Coercion: Forcing people to act in a way that they may not wish to act by use of threat or intimidation. May include blackmail or actual violence such as torture in some cases. One mark for partial definition, e.g. where people have to do something because they are scared. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. where people behave in a way that is against their wishes because they have been bullied or intimidated or they are afraid for their life or the lives of others. (b) Describe two ways in which culture can be transmitted from one generation to another. [4] Candidates will be expected to identify and describe two from the following: â€¢ Through primary socialisation â€¢ Through secondary socialisation â€¢ Through custom â€¢ Through traditions/artefacts â€¢ Through media and technology â€¢ Through religious practices â€¢ Through teaching and learning of language â€¢ Through the activities of various agencies of socialisation, e.g. education, media, family â€¢ Rites of passage â€¢ Other reasonable responses. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). Page 9 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0495 13 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (c) Explain how coercion can be used to ensure social conformity. [6] Candidates need to demonstrate an understanding of how agencies of socialisation/social control enforce the norms and values of society. Possible answers: â€¢ Formal sanctions (e.g. death penalty) â€¢ Torture, political intimidation â€¢ Informal sanctions (e.g. scolding, name-calling) â€¢ School detentions â€¢ Being â€˜groundedâ€™ by parents â€¢ Threat of the â€˜sackâ€™ in work place â€¢ Arrest and caution by police â€¢ Ostracism by religious communities or peer group â€¢ Any other reasonable response. Band 0 No creditworthy response. Band 1 (1â€“3) Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of the concepts of coercion/ social conformity. Responses may be short and un/under developed. At the top of the band, there may be a tendency towards simplistic answers, engaging with sociological ideas without using sociological language. Alternatively, candidates may offer a wider range of weak points. Answers may be placed broadly within the concept of coercion in general. Band 2 (4â€“6) A clear and accurate explanation showing good sociological knowledge and understanding of the concepts of coercion and social conformity, which is supported by relevant examples. Sociological terms should be expected. At the top of the band candidates will use sociological terms and concepts, referring to a range of factors. Candidates will address the issue of how conformity is maintained by coercion. Page 10 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0495 13 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (d) Explain why sub-cultures are formed . subject: Sociology
question_text: Feminist sociologists maintain that despite equal opportunity laws women still face discrimination in society. There are too few women in top jobs because there is a glass ceiling which they are unable to break through. Some have suggested that women form a reserve army of labour. (a) What is meant by the term â€˜glass ceilingâ€™? [2] (b) Describe two ways women may face discrimination in employment. [4] (c) Explain how the reserve army of labour may benefit modern industrial society. [6] (d) Explain why equal opportunity laws may not achieve equality in society. [8] (e) To what extent is there a lack of opportunity for women in modern industrial society? mark_scheme_text: 3 (a) What is meant by the term â€˜glass ceilingâ€™? [2] An invisible barrier that prevents women from rising to the top jobs, regardless of their qualifications or achievements. Can also be applied to other minorities. One mark for partial definition, e.g. a barrier that holds people back. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. an invisible and unbreakable barrier that prevents people from reaching the top jobs. (b) Describe two ways women may face discrimination in employment . [4] Candidates will be expected to identify and describe two from the following: â€¢ Unequal pay â€¢ Lack of opportunity/training â€¢ Passed over for promotion â€¢ Not shortlisted â€¢ Lack of child care facilities â€¢ Being held back due to career break â€¢ May not be appointed because of cost of maternity leave to employer â€¢ More likely to be made redundant â€¢ More likely to have part-time work without the same rights/benefits as full-time workers â€¢ Any other reasonable response One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). (c) Explain how the reserve army of labour may benefit modern industrial society. [6] Candidates need to demonstrate an understanding of the concept of a reserve army of labour, i.e. surplus population of â€˜working ageâ€™ who are either unemployed or under- employed. Candidates are likely to focus on women to answer this question but other groups like illegal immigrants working for below minimum wage, child labour or worldwide army of workers on less than $2 a day would also be valid. Possible answers: â€¢ By keeping wages low â€¢ By keeping profits high â€¢ Helps to maintain flexible workforce (keeps cost of goods and services low) â€¢ Benefits western society who exploit the cheap labour of other parts of the world â€¢ Benefits men in society (feminist arguments) â€¢ Women return to home or part time work when not needed at no cost to society â€¢ Children paid less, support their families but suppress wage levels â€¢ Any other reasonable response. N.B. allow asking questions/conversation/talking for partial identification. [2] (b) Identify two problems a researcher may face in collecting qualitative data. Any two from: â€¢ Need to avoid bias â€¢ Need to know how to frame questions so as to not lead interviewees â€¢ Need to be aware of ethical standards â€¢ Need to avoid contamination evidence â€¢ Need to be objective â€¢ Need to achieve value freedom â€¢ Recording data â€¢ Planning research â€¢ Interviewer effect â€¢ Reluctance to â€˜open upâ€™ to researcher â€¢ Allow time consuming and expensive â€¢ Other reasonable response. Candidates need to demonstrate an understanding that respondents may have the opportunity to talk freely and express their personal opinions, they will be speaking in their own words, they will be able to seek clarification of questions, and researcher will have the opportunity to ask follow up questions this may result in the research being more valid. If you need additional answer paper ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. Answer Question 1 and one question from Sections B or C . The number of marks is given in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question. 2 0495/12/O/N/15 Section A: Theory and methods Answer Question 1. Source A The Glasgow media groupâ€™s work focuses on developing methods of researching the media. In â€˜Bad News for Refugeesâ€™ (2013) they describe part of their research: â€˜We spoke with seven journalists from the BBC and other news outlets, including tabloid and broadsheet newspapers. Their views were given in confidence. A journalist from one tabloid spoke of the demonisation (negative stereotyping) of asylum seekers, migrants and refugees and how they were consistently treated as a single negative group of people. Some journalists spoke of problems with the accuracy of stories. A journalist checked the facts in stories appearing in the tabloids. It was found that the immigration figures used exaggerated the number of migrants who were living in the United Kingdom.â€™ Adapted from â€˜Bad News for Refugeesâ€™ Philo, Greg; Briant, Emma; Donald, Pauline. (2013) (a) From Source A, identify the research method being used by the Glasgow Media Group. [2] (b) Identify two topics in the news, apart from refugees, that could be researched using content analysis. [2] (c) Using information from Source A, describe two ways in which media content can be biased. [4] (d) Describe two strengths of using media content in sociological research. [4] (e) Describe two strengths and two limitations of using historical documents in sociological research. [8] (f) Explain why ethical issues may be a problem when carrying out sociological research. [10] (g) To what extent does research based on media content lack validity? [15] 3 0495/12/O/N/15 Answer either Question 2 or Question 3. Section B: Culture, identity and socialisation

subject: Sociology
question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜customâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two ways rewards can be used to make people conform. [4] (b) Explain how agencies of secondary socialisation transmit norms. [6] (c) Explain why minority ethnic groups may not share the norms and values of the country they live in. [8] (d) To what extent do the working class still have a distinct social identity? [15] (e) Section C: Social inequality Sociologists disagree about the causes of poverty. Some blame society, others blame the individual. Oscar Lewis argued that poverty was caused by a â€˜culture of povertyâ€™. However, Charles Murray blamed the underclass, who he claimed were lazy and did not wish to work.
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) What is meant by the term â€˜customâ€™? One mark for partial definition, e.g. â€˜ traditional pattern of behaviour â€™ . Two marks for clear definition, e.g. â€˜ widely accepted norms that have developed over a period of time â€™ . 2

2(b) Describe two ways rewards can be used to make people conform. Possible responses: Â• praise, e.g. pat on the back, verbal â€˜well doneâ€™ brings a feel-good factor encouraging repetition; Â• promotion, e.g. working hard and being punctual makes you a good employee so you get promoted to a higher post and this incentivises workers; Â• additional benefits or remuneration, e.g. pay rises and bonuses encourage a sense of loyalty and conformity; Â• inclusion/acceptance, e.g. accepted into an elite group or gang is a positive sanction which has the effect of encouraging social solidarity and conformity; Â• merits, e.g. in school encourage children to stick to the rules; Â• in family rewards, e.g. extra pocket money, sweets encourage repetition of the norm or behaviour; Â• any other reasonable response. One mark for each way identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each way developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 10 of 18 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how agencies of secondary socialisation transmit norms. Possible answers: Â• education â€“ schools transmit norms via the hidden curriculum, e.g. assemblies, rules, teacher expectations and sanctions; Â• religion transmits norms through laws and guidelines, role models and teachings of holy book, e.g. Muhammad and the Qurâ€™an; Â• media transmit norms via role models of desirable appearance, examples of good and bad behaviour, and ridicule/censure of bad behaviour or undesirable appearance; Â• peer groups transmit norms informally via positive praise and also peer pressure, e.g. ostracism; Â• the workplace transmits norms via training or re-socialisation, also mentors; Â• any other reasonable response. Band 0 [0 marks] No creditworthy response. Band 1 [1 â€“ 3 marks] Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of the way secondary socialisation transmits norms. Responses may be short and un/under developed. At the top of the band, there may be a tendency towards simplistic answers, engaging with sociological ideas without using sociological language. Alternatively, candidates may offer a wider range of weak points. Answers may be placed broadly within the concept of secondary socialisation in general. Band 2 [4 â€“ 6 marks] A clear and accurate explanation showing good sociological knowledge and understanding of the way secondary socialisation transmits norms supported by relevant examples. Sociological terms should be expected. At the top of the band candidates will use sociological terms and concepts, referring to a range of factors. Candidates will address how secondary socialisation transmits norms. 6 0495/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 11 of 18 Question Answer

subject: Sociology
question_text: A famous study by Elton Mayo was conducted in the USA in the 1920â€™s. The study was done in the Hawthorne factory. Mayo was asked to observe a group of workers and conduct a series of experiments. This was to allow the owners of the factory to create the best possible working conditions for them to get the most amount of work completed by their employees. To do this Mayo experimented with: â€¢ the levels of heating â€¢ the levels of lighting â€¢ the length of rest breaks. Mayo discovered that, no matter how the working conditions changed, the amount of work completed always increased. The possible explanations for this were: 1. The working conditions make little difference to the way people work. 2. Mayoâ€™s presence changed the behaviour of the workers. From the evidence in Source A, identify the two research methods being used. [2] (a) Identify two problems researchers might have when interviewing people about their job. [2] (b) Using information from Source A, describe two factors affecting the validity of the research. [4] (c) Describe two strengths of using telephone questionnaires in sociological research. [4] (d) Describe two strengths and two limitations of structured interviews. [8] (e) Explain why findings from participant observation may be valid. [10] (f) To what extent is the functionalist view of society correct? [15] (g) 11_0495_12_2018_1.10 2 Answer either Question 2 or Question 3. Section B: Culture, identity and socialisation Some researchers have claimed that traditional working class identity has declined because of the decrease in manual work. Other researchers have claimed there is now a new working class who have a clear social identity.
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) From the evidence in Source A, identify the two research methods being used. Acceptable responses: Â• observation/participant observation/overt participant observation; Â• experiment/field experiments. One mark for each method correctly identified from Source A (up to a maximum of two ). 2

1(b) Identify two problems researchers might have when interviewing people about their job. Possible answers: â€¢ interviewer bias/effect; â€¢ time-consuming; â€¢ need to maintain confidentiality and privacy; â€¢ need to maintain anonymity to prevent consequences from employers; â€¢ recording and analysing the data can be complex; â€¢ individuals may not tell the truth e.g. because it may involve the use of personal data such as income; â€¢ interviewees may be suspicious and not want to participate i.e. it may be difficult to gain trust; â€¢ in the formal atmosphere of the workplace an employee may feel uncomfortable and this will negatively affect the depth, detail or honesty of respondents; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each problem correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). 2 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 4 of 23 Question Answer Marks

1(c) Using information from Source A, describe two factors affecting the validity of the research. Possible answers: â€¢ the Hawthorne Effect â€“ Mayoâ€™s presence changed the behaviour of the workers and thus a true picture may not have emerged; â€¢ the study took place in a naturalistic environment â€“ the factory â€“ and this is likely to lead to more natural behaviour on the part of the workers; â€¢ Mayo couldnâ€™t control all the variables even when he thought he could e.g. he changed the lighting and length of breaks but he wouldnâ€™t be able to change factors like how much sleep the workers had or illness levels, etc.; â€¢ workerâ€™s perception of why Mayo was there made them suspicious (the ownerâ€™s requested the experiments) and made them more productive which may have given a false picture; â€¢ the research took place in the 1920s â€“ it is therefore outdated and may not present an accurate picture of how working conditions affect productivity today; â€¢ as Mayo did not participate as a worker he may not be able to fully understand their actions and therefore results may not be fully valid; â€¢ the working conditions made little difference to the way people worked â€“ suggesting that Mayoâ€™s presence affected the workersâ€™ behaviour; â€¢ the findings of observation may be subjective as the researcher selects and interprets what they see which may then be biased; â€¢ any other reasonable answer. One mark for each point correctly identified from the source (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 5 of 23 Question Answer Marks

1(d) Describe two strengths of using telephone questionnaires in sociological research. Possible answers: â€¢ not time-consuming compared to other methods

subject: Sociology
question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜social identityâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two processes through which children learn social expectations. [4] (b) Explain how conformity is rewarded in the workplace. [6] (c) Explain why individuals from different social classes may not experience the same socialisation as each other. [8] (d) To what extent is social class the most important factor in creating social identity? [15] (e) Section C: Social inequality The welfare state is viewed by some sociologists as a cause of poverty. This is because they believe it makes people unwilling to work. Critics of this view say there are a large number of people who are willing to work but find themselves in a poverty trap.
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) What is meant by the term 'social identity'? One mark for partial definition e.g. â€˜ who you think you areâ€™. Two marks for clear definition e.g. â€˜ an individualâ€™s perception of themselves based in part on how others see themâ€™. 2

2(b) Describe two processes through which children learn social expectations. Possible responses: â€¢ manipulation or how parents and others encourage some behaviours and discourage others e.g. encouraging boys to be rough and girls to be gentle; â€¢ verbal appellation or using language to reinforce gender stereotypes e.g. â€˜my brave little soldierâ€™, â€˜my little princessâ€™, etc.; â€¢ primary socialisation â€“ the family teaches expected norms and values of the culture and reinforces it with informal social controls; â€¢ imitation i.e. copying the behaviour of role models such as parents, peers, teachers, etc.; â€¢ canalisation â€“ boys are channelled towards male behaviour like playing football to learn expected gender roles, often through toys; â€¢ secondary socialisation e.g. the hidden curriculum teaches obedience to the people above you in the hierarchical structure of the school; â€¢ peer group pressure â€“ both negative such as ostracism and bullying, and positive such as praise and status; â€¢ media techniques such as stereotyping representations can affect gender and other identities; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each process correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each process developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 12 of 23 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how conformity is rewarded in the workplace. Possible responses: â€¢ conformity to expected roles may be shown informally by approval e.g. smiles, pat on the back from the boss, high five from team members etc.; â€¢ doing the right thing may be rewarded by verbal praise e.g. â€˜well doneâ€™, â€˜good jobâ€™, etc. gives a feel-good factor and promotes repetition; â€¢ conformity brings a feeling of fitting in and may be rewarded informally by inclusion and acceptance e.g. invitation to after work outing with peers and/or superiors; â€¢ conforming to expectations in the workplace can be rewarded formally by a pay increase e.g. teachers following certain standards; â€¢ conforming to the norms and values in a workplace may lead to promotion as workers are seen as good role models and mentors for others; â€¢ conformity may be rewarded via other â€˜perksâ€™ such as a new company car or a bonus; â€¢ any other reasonable response. Band 0 [0 marks] No creditworthy response. Band 1 [1â€“3 marks] Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of the way conformity is rewarded. Responses may be short and un/under developed. At the top of the band, there may be a tendency towards simplistic answers, engaging with sociological ideas without using sociological language. Alternatively, candidates may offer a wider range of weak points. Answers may be placed broadly within the concept of social conformity

3(c) Explain how sociologists measure poverty. Possible answers: â€¢ absolute poverty i.e. not having the resources necessary to support life such as money, shelter, access to clean water, sanitation, food and medicine; â€¢ relative poverty i.e. poor by the standards of the society as a whole; â€¢ using a poverty line i.e. an artificial line below which people can be classed as poor e.g. income of less than 60% of the median income (Townsend); â€¢ a deprivation index e.g. creating a list of items thought to be necessities in that society and then measuring how many items individuals cannot access (Mack and Lansleyâ€™s Breadline Britain survey); â€¢ global measures of poverty e.g. the World Bankâ€™s measure of poverty as living on less than $1.25 a day; â€¢ any other reasonable response. 2(b) Describe two ways rewards can be used to make people conform. subject: Sociology
question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜communeâ€™? (a) [2] Describe two types of cohabitation. [4] (b) Explain how urbanisation has affected family life. (c) [6] Explain why the symmetrical family has become more common in modern industrial societies. (d) [8] To what extent are nuclear families patriarchal? (e) [15] Section B: Education In modern industrial societies many different types of schools exist, for example private schools. All types of schools function to help students pass exams as well as prepare them for the world of work. For this reason, schools are an important agency of socialisation.

1(b) Describe two types of cohabitation. Possible answers: â€¢ a trial marriage â€“ living together before marriage; â€¢ a short-term relationship without real commitment; â€¢ a permanent or long-term relationship â€“ the same as a marriage just without the ceremony; â€¢ two heterosexuals living together (with or without children); â€¢ two homosexuals living together (with or without children); â€¢ other reasonable response. 1(c) Explain how urbanisation has affected family life. Possible answers: â€¢ traditional large extended family units have reduced and been replaced by smaller family units such as the nuclear family; â€¢ functions that were once performed by the family i.e. care of the young and the elderly are now performed by the state and its institutions; â€¢ face to face contact with wider family members is thought to be less than previously, replaced in part by technology e.g. email, skype, facetime, social media, etc.; â€¢ young adults tend to move to cities leaving rural areas with mainly older people/children; â€¢ families living very close to other families in shanty towns; â€¢ nuclear families tend to live further away from their extended families due to the need to be geographically mobile; â€¢ in times of crisis the extended family are unlikely to be the source of help and support that they once were as now the welfare state steps in; â€¢ living in cities means the cost of living is likely to be higher and so families are likely to be smaller to make life more affordable; â€¢ family life has become privatised (particularly in the nuclear family) with the home being at the centre of family life â€“ this has led to factors such as child centredness/symmetry, etc.; â€¢ other reasonable response. Band 0 [0 marks] No creditworthy response. Band 1 [1â€“3 marks] Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of how urbanisation has affected family life and may talk about e.g. â€˜ families living apartâ€™ . 2(c) Explain how a schoolâ€™s use of IQ tests could affect a childâ€™s experience of education. question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜status frustrationâ€™? (a) [2] Describe two reasons why the police might not record a crime that has been reported to them. (d) [8] To what extent do victim surveys provide an accurate measure of crime? (e) [15] Section D: Media Sociologists debate how the audience is affected by the content of the media. Some believe the uses and gratifications model is the most accurate. Whilst representations in the media are changing, many sociologists argue that it is still important to consider if media content is stereotypical.

question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜social mobilityâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two ways that schools socially control pupils. [15] (e) 11_0495_22_2018_1.11 2 Section C: Crime, deviance and social control There are different ways that sociologists can measure crime, for example self-report studies. However using these measurements to explain why people commit crime causes much debate. What is meant by the term â€˜self-report studiesâ€™? [2] (a)

2(b) Describe two ways that schools socially control pupils. Possible answers: â€¢ Through formal social control â€“ exclusions and school rules; â€¢ Through informal social control â€“ warnings; â€¢ Through negative sanctions such as detentions â€“ fear of punishment; â€¢ Through deterrence â€“ punishment puts children off from misbehaving again; â€¢ Through encouraging conformity â€“ children like to fit in; â€¢ Through positive sanctions such as stickers and praise â€“ this encourages children to continue behaving in the â€˜rightâ€™ way; â€¢ Through the hidden curriculum â€“ Marxist ideas of ideological control; â€¢ Through the patriarchal curriculum â€“ feminist ideas of male control; â€¢ Through positive or negative peer pressure â€“ anti-school or pro-school sub-cultures; â€¢ Through secondary socialisation processes â€“ teachers teaching the expected norms and values for later life; â€¢ Other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). 4 0495/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2018 Page 12 of 32 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how positive discrimination can improve the educational achievement of some social groups. Possible answers: â€¢ Some schools have introduced schemes to encourage girls to aim higher, especially in science and technology subjects (STEM policies, GIST, WISE, etc.). Girls may be given preferential access to lab equipment, for example; â€¢ Single-sex classes within co-educational schools â€“ thought to improve the performance of both males and females; â€¢ Lessons geared towards boysâ€™ ways of learning (active learning) have been introduced in many schools to enable boys to fulfil their academic potential; â€¢ Ethnic minority pupils may be given small group/individual/additional lessons in English as an additional or secondary language to boost educational achievement; â€¢ Some universities and private schools are now setting quotas that ensure they admit pupils from disadvantaged backgrounds â€“ often with lower results than the average; â€¢ Financial bursaries, scholarships, policies that give financial aid/resources to students and incentives are often available to benefit and support those from socioeconomically deprived and/or ethnic minority backgrounds; â€¢ Extra-curricular, additional and holiday lessons/summer schools are run by many schools for less privileged social groups to attend; â€¢ Educational priority zones/ZEPs can receive better funding and additional resources to improve educational achievement of deprived individuals; â€¢ Other reasonable response. [15] (e) Section B: Education In modern industrial societies many different types of schools exist, for example private schools. All types of schools function to help students pass exams as well as prepare them for the world of work. For this reason, schools are an important agency of socialisation.

1(b) Describe two types of cohabitation. subject: Sociology
question_text: Devising sociological research can be affected by various factors such as: the researcherâ€™s social identity, their personal experiences and interests as well as their theoretical perspective and preferences for particular sociological approaches. Other factors affecting research design include: ethical issues, time constraints, accessing samples and funding. Doing research can be expensive so getting funding is important. Many sociologists get their funding from governments or charities. Although all parts of the research process are important pilot studies are considered essential by many researchers. A pilot study is a preliminary small scale study that is carried out before the main research. It can help researchers decide how to carry out a large scale study as well as identify potential problems with the method, sample or research question. From Source A , identify two ways sociologists get their funding. [2] (a) Identify two examples of sampling frames used by sociologists. [2] (b) Using information from Source A , describe two reasons why researchers like to use pilot studies. [4] (c) Describe two strengths of using semi-structured interviews in sociological research. [4] (d) Describe two strengths and two limitations of using snowball sampling in sociological research. [8] (e) Explain why sociologists should follow ethical guidelines when carrying out research. [10] (f) To what extent are positivist methods the most effective way of carrying out sociological research? [15] (g) 06_0495_11_2019_1.1 2 Answer either Question 2 or Question

Possible answers: Â• using a pilot study enables researchers to identify potential problems with the method â€“ e.g. whether it gives appropriate data; Â• using a pilot study enables researchers to identify potential problems with the research question e.g. whether itâ€™s well focused; Â• using a pilot study enables researchers to identify potential problems with the sampling procedure and make sure the sample is appropriate â€“ issues of access and representativeness can be overcome; Â• using a pilot study enables researchers to prepare for a large scale study - these are expensive so a small-scale pilot study is essential to iron out any initial problems with methods or research devices before undertaking the full research; Â• a successful pilot study is useful in securing funding as the study is deemed viable; Â• a pilot study helps to identify ethical and practical issues which might occur; Â• a pilot study can be helpful in determining what resources (finance, staff) are needed for a planned study; Â• a pilot study can ensure the research question is appropriate and likely to find the information required; Â• a pilot study is useful in order to make sure the theoretical perspective of the researcher wonâ€™t impact upon the respondents; Â• any other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified from the source (up to a maximum of two ). subject: Sociology
question_text: Feminist researchers are often concerned that the balance of power between the researcher and respondent is likely to affect any data gathered. They argue that the interviewer and the interviewee should be seen as equals in the research process. Feminists prefer unstructured interviews which give time and space for the interviewee to offer their views. In structured interviews the interviewer is the one in control, they direct the questioning and record the data. In unstructured interviews the interviewees describe their experiences in their own words with the interviewer acting only as a guide. This is why feminists prefer to use unstructured interviews. Feminists also like to use focus groups in their research. Positivists select objective methods that avoid interviewer bias but feminists reject this approach. Instead feminists claim that developing a relationship with respondents is an essential part of establishing trust and respect. Positivists also argue that unstructured interviews are very difficult to repeat. From Source A , identify two research methods used by feminists. [2] (a) Identify two types of observation used by sociologists. [2] (b) Using information from Source A , describe two reasons why some sociologists like to use unstructured interviews. [4] (c) Describe two strengths of using a social survey in sociological research. [4] (d) Describe two strengths and two limitations of using focus groups in sociological research. [8] (e) Explain why sampling is important for sociological research. [10] (f) To what extent is validity the most important aspect of sociological research? [15] (g) 06_0495_12_2019_1.15 2 Answer either Question 2 or Question
mark_scheme_text:

question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜informal social controlâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two examples of norms expected of students in schools. Ways of regulating behaviour imposed by people without a formal role to do so (such as peers), through informal means such as expectations and praise. One mark for partial definition e.g. Parent telling you off for being naughty. Two marks for clear definition e.g. Informal social control refers to the ways in which the family, peers, media etc. ensure our conformity to the norms and values of the society. 2

2(b) Describe two examples of norms of students expected in schools. Possible answers: Two from: Â• wearing uniform â€“ to show group identity in school; Â• respect for teachers and fellow pupils e.g. not talking over others; Â• punctuality â€“ e.g. avoid lateness so you donâ€™t face sanctions; Â• being prepared for learning e.g. having all the right equipment with you; Â• using appropriate language e.g. no swearing or abusive language in school; Â• concentrating in lessons e.g. you may be punished if you get distracted; Â• completing homework e.g. complete tasks given to as to avoid sanctions; Â• any other reasonable response. 2(c) Explain how young people may experience role conflict. Possible answers: Â• role conflict between paid work and student may mean young people fall behind with school work because of work pressures; Â• role conflict between student and peer group member â€“ students may want to get on at school but fear losing credibility with their peer group and hence fall prey to negative peer pressure; Â• role conflict between the role of daughter/son and girlfriend/boyfriend â€“ young people can face competing demands on the emotional time and this may result in a distancing from the family members as more social time is spent with boy/girlfriends; Â• role conflict between sister/brother and friend â€“ older siblings may be expected to look after younger siblings when theyâ€™d rather be out with their friends and this may result in clashes with parents; Â• any other reasonable response. question_text: Devising sociological research can be affected by various factors such as: the researcherâ€™s social identity, their personal experiences and interests as well as their theoretical perspective and preferences for particular sociological approaches. Other factors affecting research design include: ethical issues, time constraints, accessing samples and funding. Doing research can be expensive so getting funding is important. Many sociologists get their funding from governments or charities. Although all parts of the research process are important pilot studies are considered essential by many researchers. [Turn over 06_0495_11_2020_1.15 *1552497353* Section A: Theory and methods Answer Question Results from a content analysis of the working roles of women on UK television Fig. 1. Content analysis is used to research the media. This method measures the number of times a particular representation occurs. For example, a researcher may use content analysis to count the number of times women are represented in working roles on television. This quantitative approach to analysing media content involves designing a classification grid of womenâ€™s working roles on television (as Fig.1.1). The researcher then counts how many times women appear in these roles in a sample of different programmes. To carry out content analysis it is important to work in a standardised way. This means all researchers must know exactly what to count and to make sure they are counting the same thing and recording their data in the same way. From Source A , identify the two types of programme in which the â€˜Housewifeâ€™ role was most common. [2] (a) Identify two methods, apart from content analysis, that could be used to gain quantitative data. [2] (b) Using information from Source A , describe two reasons why it is important for researchers to work in a standardised way. [4] (c) Describe two limitations of using content analysis in sociological research. [4] (d) Describe two strengths and two limitations of using triangulation in sociological research. [8] (e) Explain why some sociologists use official statistics. [10] (f) To what extent are focus groups the most effective research method? [15] (g) 06_0495_11_2020_1.15
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) From Source A, identify the two types of programme in which the â€˜Housewifeâ€™ role was most common. Acceptable responses: â€¢ soap â€¢ drama One mark for each method correctly identified from Source A (up to a maximum of two ). 2

1(b) Identify two methods, apart from content analysis, that could be used to gain quantitative data. Possible answers: â€¢ field experiments/experiments â€¢ non-participant observation â€¢ questionnaire/survey â€¢ structured interview â€¢ semi-structured interview â€¢ Any other reasonable response One mark for each method correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). 2

1(c) Using information from Source A, describe two reasons why it is important for researchers to work in a standardised way. Possible answers: â€¢ working in a standardised is an important aspect of the quantitative approach â€¢ as it produces data that can be usefully compared â€¢ using a classification grid to count representations of women enables the researcher to remain objective â€¢ working in a standardised way , counting the number of a times a representation occurs is a scientific approach to research and an approach respected by positivists . â€¢ counting the same thing and recording their data in the same way will ensure the method is high in reliability â€¢ counting how many times women appear in these roles in a sample of different programmes will allow researchers to detect patterns and trends â€¢ Any other reasonable answer One mark for each reason correctly identified from the source (up to a maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). 4 0495/11 Update field for Qualification Level â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 4 of 19 Question Answer Marks

1(d) Describe two limitations of using content analysis in sociological research. Possible answers: â€¢ it is far from objective. Researchers generate their own categories from which to code and these become second-order constructs â€¢ the researcher, usually working alone, has to place individuals in categories â€“ this is a subjective technique. â€¢ quantitative research falls to examine the ideological messages about the groups being studied â€¢ most content analysis is empirical research and not based upon a body of theory â€¢ can be extremely time consuming â€¢ is subject to increased error, particularly when semiotic analysis is used to attain a higher level of interpretation â€¢ is often devoid of theoretical base, or attempts too liberally to draw meaningful inferences about the relationships and impacts implied in a study â€¢ is inherently reductive, particularly when dealing with complex texts â€¢ tends too often to simply consist of word/image counts â€“ lacks validity â€¢ often disregards the context that produced the text â€¢ very difficult to obtain a sample that is representative of all media â€¢ Any other reasonable response. One mark for each limitation correctly identified (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/11 Updat

[Turn over 06_0495_12_2020_1.11 *8402547323* Section A: Theory and methods Answer Question The comparative study is a scientific approach to sociological research and is often used by positivists. Sociologists can compare data between social groups and identify differences between them, such as educational achievement. Sociologists may compare data such as the different female literacy rates around the world (Fig. 1.1). Female literacy rates refer to the percentage of women over 15 years of age that can read and write in a country. Another example of a comparative study is the UK census. Every ten years in the UK all households have to complete a detailed survey. Many of the questions are standardised and remain the same over time. Like many sources of official statistics the UK census gathers a large amount of quantitative data taking a macro approach. Afghanistan Chile Ghana Country International Female Literacy Rates Nicaragua Nigeria 100 90 80 70 60 50 40 30 20 10 From Source A , identify the two countries with the lowest female literacy rates. [2] (a) Identify two methods that could be used by positivists. [2] (b) Using information from Source A , describe two reasons why researchers use official statistics. [4] (c) Describe two limitations of using case studies in sociological research. [4] (d) Describe two strengths and two limitations of using unstructured interviews in sociological research. [8] (e) Explain why objectivity is difficult to maintain when carrying out sociological research. [10] (f) To what extent are macro structuralist approaches the most useful for understanding society? [15] (g) 06_0495_12_2020_1.11

1(b) Identify two methods that could be used by positivists. Possible answers: â€¢ experiment â€¢ questionnaire â€¢ survey â€¢ structured interview â€¢ content analysis â€¢ comparative studies â€¢ Any other reasonable response. One mark for each method correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). 2

1(c) Using information from Source A, describe two reasons why researchers use official statistics. Possible answers: â€¢ many of the questions are standardised â€“ this means the data that makes up official statistics will be reliably collected; â€¢ official statistical data from the UK census is taken every ten years this allows for comparisons to be made over time e.g. longitudinal studies; â€¢ quantitative data is useful for the comparative method , it enables researchers to spot trends and patterns in official statistics; â€¢ all households have to complete a detailed survey â€“ official statistics often have large samples which are representative samples; â€¢ many sources of official statistics the UK census gathers a large amount of quantitative data which improves the chances of generalisability; â€¢ any other reasonable response One mark for each reason correctly identified from Source A (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 4 of 21 Question Answer Marks

1(d) Describe two limitations of using case studies in sociological research. Possible answers: â€¢ the findings may only apply to the case â€“ so generalisations cannot be made; â€¢ the study cannot be replicated as it is unique â€“ and this limits reliability; â€¢ the deep involvement of the researchers may lead them to being influenced by their own feelings â€“ thus decreasing the validity of the findings; â€¢ case studies can be very time consuming â€“ the data collection process can be very intensive; â€¢ when conducting a case study, it is very possible for the author to form a bias/subjective view â€“ thus affecting the validity of results; â€¢ case study method may have errors of memory or judgment â€“ recorded data may not be accurate; â€¢ with small studies, there is always the question of ethics â€“ at what point do detailed and in depth studies become intrusive; â€¢ any other reasonable response One mark for each limitation correctly identified (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 5 of 21 Question Answer Marks

[15] (e) Section C: Social inequality Karl Marx argued that society was divided into two main social classes: the bourgeoisie (the upper class) and the proletariat (the working class). Class stratification and status in modern industrial 3 societies are changing and new classes have been formed, such as the underclass. [6] (c) Explain why some sociologists believe that proletarianisation has occurred in modern industrial societies. [8] (d) To what extent are Marxist explanations of social inequality the most useful? 3(b) Describe two examples of ascribed status. Possible answers: â€¢ being born male or female â€“ status is ascribed according to oneâ€™s sex at birth; â€¢ royal status â€“ people are born or marry into royalty and do not achieve this status; â€¢ sister/brother â€“ the birth of a sibling is beyond the individualâ€™s control; â€¢ age related statuses â€“ though these change throughout oneâ€™s life age related status is not achieved; â€¢ ethnic related statuses â€“ being a ethnic minority or majority is an ascribed status largely beyond the control of the individual; â€¢ any other reasonable response One mark for each example of ascribed status identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each description (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 17 of 21 Question Answer Marks

3(c) Explain how the underclass is different from other social classes. Possible answers: â€¢ cultural differences â€“ the underclass have a way of life characterised by a dependency culture; â€¢ different lifestyle choices â€“ these can lead to social exclusion resulting in unemployment; â€¢ different attitudes to work and achievement â€“ underclass are characterised by fatalism and despair; â€¢ higher crime rates â€“ the underclass may turn to crime as they have more blocked opportunities than the middle class; â€¢ different attitude to education â€“ leads to limited life chances in later life; â€¢ higher proportion of teen pregnancies and single parent households â€“ these positions are often associated with a dependency on state benefits; â€¢ material deprivation â€“ the underclass have less money and access to goods than the middle class; â€¢ poverty trap â€“ the underclass are more likely to be stuck in the poverty trap because of their attitudes to work and education; â€¢ ethnic differences â€“ in the USA the underclass are associated with ethnic minority groups; â€¢ any other reasonable response. Band 2 [4â€“6 marks] Answers in this band will have clear and accurate explanation, showing good sociological knowledge and understanding. mark_scheme_text:
3(a) What is meant by the term â€˜relative deprivationâ€™? One mark for partial definition, e.g. being poor. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. the feeling of having less than others in a society. 2

3(b) Describe two agencies of social control. Candidates will describe two different agencies of social control, these can be either formal or informal or a mixture of both. Possible answers: â€¢ the police â€“ control through the power of arrest, surveillance and their presence; â€¢ the Government â€“ control through the making and implementation and enforcement of the law; â€¢ the penal system â€“ control through incarceration/prison, taking away freedom and choices; â€¢ the judiciary â€“ control through sentencing, deciding if someone is guilty or innocent and, if guilty, how best to sentence them; â€¢ the family â€“ control through sanctions, imitation, gender roles (canalisation etc.); â€¢ education and schools â€“ control through negative and positive sanctions, the hidden curriculum etc.; â€¢ the workplace â€“ control through promotion, dismissal, paying wages, re- socialisation; â€¢ the peer group â€“ control through peers wanting to fit in, peer pressure, role models; â€¢ the media â€“ control through representations and stereotypes, moral panics, imitation, agenda setting, norm referencing etc.; â€¢ religion â€“ control through the ultimate sanctions of heaven and hell, dress codes, dietary requirements, prayer etc.; â€¢ other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). 4 0495/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 19 of 33 Question Answer Marks

3(c) Explain how Marxists view crime. Candidates should show an understanding of the Marxist view of crime. This can also include Left Realist explanations such as Lea and Youngâ€™s. Possible answers: â€¢ laws are made in the interests of the ruling class and criminalise the actions of the working class â€“ this is why there are so many more working class criminals; â€¢ white-collar and corporate crime is hidden in the OCS as the agents of social control focus their attentions on the lower classes; â€¢ police, as agents of the state, target and arrest the working class far more than the ruling class meaning they are over-represented in the OCS and the prison population; â€¢ working class crime as an expression of resistance against the structural inequalities of society; â€¢ material deprivation â€“ poverty leads to crime; â€¢ relative deprivation â€“ in a consumer driven society material possessions become highly valued but not everyone can afford them, hence crime occurs; â€¢ status frustration, marginalisation and alienation â€“ lower social classes and some ethnic minorities do not feel part of wider society so have no other outlet for their frustrations and anger than rioting, vandalism and crime; â€¢ Marxists believe that capitalism causes crime; â€¢ Marxists believe that the police operate a system of selective law enforcement

subject: Sociology
question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜gender equalityâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two trends in divorce in modern industrial societies. [4] (b) Explain how ethnicity can affect family life. [6] (c) Explain why the average family size has decreased in modern industrial societies. [8] (d) To what extent are family roles changing in modern industrial societies? [15] (e) Section B: Education Many sociologists believe that how well an individual does in education is not down to their efforts or IQ but more to do with social characteristics such as ethnicity, gender and social class. This can 2 be explained by looking at both school and home factors. Other sociologists believe that academic and vocational education are meritocratic and open to everyone. What is meant by the term â€˜home factorsâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two examples of vocational education. [4] (b) Explain how private schools may reproduce social inequalities. [6] (c) Explain why some ethnic groups do less well than others in education. [8] (d) To what extent is the feminist view of education accurate? [15] (e) 06_0495_22_2020_1.9
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) What is meant by the term â€˜gender equalityâ€™? One mark for partial definition, e.g. womenâ€™s rights. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. when men and women have equal roles, status and rights. 2

1(b) Describe two trends in divorce in modern industrial societies. Candidates will describe two trends in divorce in MISs. A trend is any pattern that can be seen when it comes to divorce. Statistics may be used but are not essential. Possible answers: â€¢ age â€“ those who marry young are more likely to divorce; â€¢ class â€“ middle class people are less likely to divorce than working class people; â€¢ society â€“ the divorce rate is high in MISâ€™s as compared to other types of society; â€¢ patterns â€“ currently the divorce rate has flattened off suggesting it is no longer rising; â€¢ gender â€“ women are more likely to file for divorce than men; â€¢ religion â€“ those with strong religious beliefs are less likely to divorce than those who are non-religious; â€¢ era -in the UK the divorce rate is three times higher today than in 1969; â€¢ country â€“ the UK has the highest divorce rate in Europe â€“ in 2010 120 000 divorces took place; â€¢ gender â€“ women in the UK are two and a half times more likely to be granted a divorce compared to men; â€¢ other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). 4 0495/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 4 of 32 Question Answer Marks

1(c) Explain how ethnicity can affect family life. Candidates can use ethnicity in many different ways â€“ as long as this is linked to family life it is fine to credit. They may well draw on local, cultural examples. Typical areas of discussion may be family roles, family types, family size etc. Possible answers: â€¢ ethnic minority families typically adapt to the country they are living in whilst still holding onto cultural practices from their country of origin (dual identity); â€¢ Afro-Caribbean families may have high rates of single parents and are typically matrifocal; â€¢ South Asian families are often extended in form, have low divorce rates and few instances of single parenthood; â€¢ arranged marriages are practised by some ethnic groups as the norm e.g. some South Asian families; â€¢ for some ethnic groups the law determines aspects of family life e.g. Chinese families may be influenced by Chinaâ€™s â€˜one childâ€™ policy; â€¢ South Asian families are often patriarchal in nature with the males (father and brothers) often having more power and influence than the females; â€¢ South Asian families often abide by traditional segregated conjugal roles â€“ male breadwinner and female housewife/mother; â€¢ cultural defence â€“ some families may socialise children into their cultural norms and values rather than the host nations; â€¢ dual heritage/mixed race families â€“ hybridity may be seen here when it comes to roles and experiences in the family; â€¢ for some ethnic groups marital practices such as polygamy are

If you need additional answer paper, ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. INFORMATION â€¢ The total mark for this paper is 70. â€¢ The number of marks for each question or part question is shown in brackets [ ]. This document has 4 pages. Blank pages are indicated. [Turn over 06_0495_22_2020_1.9 *1604701368* Answer two questions. Section A: Family Whether gender equality in the family exists is a matter of debate. Many factors affect this, for example social characteristics such as ethnicity and social class. Also important are the type and size of the family as well as the roles family members play. Section C: Crime, deviance and social control There are official and non-official statistics that measure the crime rate in a society. However, not all crimes are accurately represented in the statistics suggesting that some groups are less likely 3 than others to be prosecuted for their crimes. Some believe there is a need for more formal social control and harsher punishments to deal with the high levels of offending. What is meant by the term â€˜formal social controlâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two punishments for crime. [4] (b) Explain how poverty can be linked to crime. [6] (c) Explain why white-collar crime is under-reported and under-recorded in the official crime statistics. [8] (d) To what extent are self-report studies the most accurate measurement of crime? [15] (e) Section D: Media The media is often accused of exaggerated and sensationalist content, particularly in the news. This can be due to the pressures of advertising, issues of agenda setting and news values. This 4 has led to the argument that the media and the news are biased. However, bias in the media may be reducing due to increased levels of audience interactivity and social media. What is meant by the term â€˜biasâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two ways media content can be censored. [4] (b) Explain how people interact using social media. [6] (c) Explain why the media is an important agency of social control. [8] (d) To what extent does the news reflect reality? [15] (e) 06_0495_22_2020_1.9

2(a) What is meant by the term â€˜home factorsâ€™? One mark for partial definition, e.g. family factors. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. things in the home background of children (family or community) that affect how they do in school. 2

2(b) Describe two examples of vocational education. Candidates will describe two examples of vocational education. This could be to do with the skills learnt, the qualifications or the institution but must be linked to preparing people for work in some way. Specific examples may be referred to. Possible answers: â€¢ skills â€“ where students are prepared for work e.g. ICT; â€¢ training â€“ training students for a particular job or career e.g. hairdressing; â€¢ courses â€“ vocational courses offered in secondary schools or further education institutions e.g. BTECâ€™s; â€¢ apprenticeships â€“ e.g. training students to be car mechanics; â€¢ workplace â€“ on the job training; â€¢ other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). 4 0495/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 12 of 32 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how private schools may reproduce social inequalities. Candidates should consider how private schools may reproduce social inequalities. The Marxist theory may be referred to. Possible answers: â€¢ private schools are fee-paying therefore they exclude those who canâ€™t afford them from an elite education thus reproducing social inequalities; â€¢ many private schools are single-sex with a lot being for boys â€“ this can mean a continuation of patriarchal institutions such as the â€˜old boys network that reproduce social inequalities (social capital); â€¢ private schools typically get the best examination results which benefits their students in terms of life chances and so reproduces social inequalities; â€¢ private schools are typically selective and it is argued that having cultural capital (associated with the higher classes in society) aids passing the entrance exam â€“ this cultural capital reproduces social inequalities as more of the higher classes get accepted; â€¢ Marxists believe that education is another institution controlled by the bourgeoisie and therefore private education ensures through social closure that the elite maintain their privileged position in society; â€¢ going to private school is seen to be advantageous to attending elite universities and so reproducing social inequalities as this is likely to be the higher classes (economic capital); â€¢ other reasonable response. Band 0 [0 marks] No creditworthy response. Band 1 [1â€“3 marks] Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of how private schools may reproduce social inequalities and may talk about e.g. â€˜ private schools get better results than state schools so people who go there do better in lifeâ€™. 3(a) What is meant by the term â€˜formal social controlâ€™? One mark for partial definition, e.g. the police. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. formal social control is the ways the Government or its agencies get people to conform to the law. 2

3(b) Describe two punishments for crime. Candidates will describe two different punishments for crime â€“ these could be formal or informal. Possible answers: â€¢ a prison sentence â€“ takes away a personâ€™s freedom; â€¢ the death sentence â€“ for the most serious crimes the punishment may be death; â€¢ corporal punishment â€“ some societies punish criminals physically e.g. with lashes; â€¢ community sentencing â€“ non-custodial sentences that help the community in some way; â€¢ exile or ostracism â€“ the offender has to leave or is excluded from their community; â€¢ tagging â€“ electronic tags can be used to monitor a personâ€™s behaviour and whereabouts; â€¢ fine â€“ a monetary sanction designed to punish offenders financially; â€¢ other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). 4 0495/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 19 of 32 Question Answer Marks

3(c) Explain how poverty can be linked to crime. Candidates should show an understanding of how poverty can be linked to crime. Specific examples may be referred to as may the Marxist view. Possible answers: â€¢ relative deprivation â€“ when individuals see themselves as deprived in relation to others crime may occur (Merton strain theory); â€¢ marginalisation â€“ when groups have no organisations to represent and protect their interests therefore may turn to crime to vent their frustrations and anger at society (Lea and Young); â€¢ in countries with no/inadequate welfare systems, crime may be the only way to survive; â€¢ Cloward and Ohlin believed that in deprived and poor communities individuals engaged in criminal or deviant behaviour to be part of the illegitimate opportunity structure when mainstream society offered them few possibilities for legitimate success; â€¢ poorer people are more likely to be labelled as criminal and are thus targeted more by the formal agents e.g. stop and search/arrest rates; â€¢ deviant careers â€“ the effects of public labelling can devastate a person and give them little choice but to adopt a life of crime; â€¢ Cohen â€“ status frustration â€“ poorer people may find it difficult to achieve status in legitimate means in society and so may turn to crime for this instead; â€¢ Marxists believe that capitalism causes crime as it creates an unequal society that keeps poor people poor and rich people rich and sees a growing gap in inequalities; â€¢ the media often turn the poor into folk devils and blame them for criminal activities in society; â€¢ individuals may turn to crime in response to these negative labels; â€¢ the underclass â€“ Charles Murray â€“ are said to have a distinct set of deviant values which can be linked to criminality; â€¢ other reasonabl

[Turn over 06_0495_23_2020_1.8 *5997183220* Answer two questions. Section A: Family Some sociologists claim that dysfunctional families cause problems in society. Other sociologists take a more positive view and believe that families are important for functions such as socialisation 1 and social control. In reality, it depends upon the type of family an individual lives in and the roles that grandparents, parents and children are expected to play. What is meant by the term â€˜dysfunctional familyâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two examples of child-centeredness. [4] (b) Explain how grandparents can play important roles in family life. [6] (c) Explain why the divorce rate is high in modern industrial societies. [8] (d) To what extent are nuclear families the best type of family to live in today? [15] (e) Section B: Education Sociologists debate whether cultural deprivation, labelling and material factors limit educational success. Education is not just about gaining qualifications and measuring intelligence, it also has many other functions such as reinforcing social expectations. What is meant by the term â€˜labellingâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two ways social expectations are reinforced in school. [4] (b) Explain how cultural deprivation may be a barrier to educational success. [6] (c) Explain why IQ tests are criticised as a way of measuring intelligence. [8] (d) To what extent do peer groups influence educational achievement? [15] (e) 06_0495_23_2020_1.8 Section C: Crime, deviance and social control Crime rates differ depending upon the type of crime, where it is committed and the way it is measured. The rates of offending and conviction vary depending on the social characteristics of 3 the offender. Many explanations exist for this, including police targeting and the role of the media and new technologies. When crime rates rise governments and the public demand more social control. Some sociologists argue that this will not reduce crime. What is meant by the term â€˜targetingâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two ways of measuring crime. [4] (b) Explain how crime can be linked to new technologies. [6] (c) Explain why crime rates for some ethnic groups are higher than others. [8] (d) To what extent can formal social control reduce crime? [15] (e) Section D: Media In recent years the media has changed due to factors such as the growth of the internet, diversification and new types of funding. Not everyone believes that this has affected the way social 4 groups are represented in the media. Stereotyping, for example, can still
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) What is meant by the term â€˜labellingâ€™? One mark for partial definition, e.g. a stereotype. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. defining a person or group in a particular way so that certain behaviour is expected of them. 2

2(b) Describe two ways social expectations are reinforced in school. Candidates will describe two ways that social expectations are reinforced in school. Candidates are likely to consider the role of education here and so may refer to functionalism and/or Marxism. This is not a necessity however. Possible answers: â€¢ education is an agency of secondary socialisation whose â€˜roleâ€™ is to teach children what society expects of them (the hidden curriculum); â€¢ school is an agency of social control so positive sanctions/rewards are used to teach children what the â€˜correctâ€™ way to behave and think is; â€¢ school as an agency of social control also uses negative sanctions/punishments to ensure that children do not engage in deviant behaviour; â€¢ negative sanctions can also act as a deterrent to prevent children straying from the correct path; â€¢ Marxists believe that those in power use schools as an agent of the state to control the minds and actions of the future workforce and so make them compliant and obedient; â€¢ feminism â€“ gendered subject choice in schools can be seen to reinforce patriarchal expectations in society of the roles of men and women e.g. encouraging females to study â€˜communicationâ€™ subjects and males technical; â€¢ schools can be seen to reinforce a collective culture through shared acts and celebrations e.g. patriotism, learning about the countryâ€™s heritage and history etc.; â€¢ functionalists believe that education is meritocratic and so at school children learn that through hard work and effort they can achieve their status; â€¢ other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). 4 0495/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 11 of 33 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how cultural deprivation may be a barrier to educational success. Candidates should consider how cultural deprivation may be a barrier to educational success. CULTURAL DEPRIVATION â€“ aspects of the values and attitudes from home of some children prevents them from achieving highly in education. Possible answers: â€¢ a fatalistic attitude amongst the working class â€“ not believing that you can change your future if you work hard; â€¢ a value placed on immediate rather than deferred gratification â€“ working class being unable to see the benefits of putting off rewards and staying in education; â€¢ working class boys are said to have a thrill-seeking culture which may get them into trouble and may lead to the formation of anti-school subcultures; â€¢ having parents who do not value education â€“ they may have had a negative experience themselves; â€¢ Bourdieuâ€™s cultural capital â€“ not having this capital may be a reason as to why students do
