3(a) What is meant by the term â€˜targetingâ€™? One mark for partial definition, e.g. police stereotypes. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. when the police focus on a particular group of people, believing them to be more likely to be involved in criminal behaviour than others. 2

3(b) Describe two ways of measuring crime. Candidates will describe two ways of measuring crime. Specific examples may be used to support the ideas and these may be from a local context. Possible answers: â€¢ official statistics â€“ the official government figures from the police and courts to measure the number of crimes and offenders; â€¢ self-report studies â€“ research which asks people what crimes they have committed; â€¢ victim surveys â€“ research that asks people what crimes they have been victims of; â€¢ national victim surveys â€“ as above but done on a national scale e.g. the CSEW to measure patterns of crime within a country; â€¢ local victim surveys e.g. left realist research â€“ focused on a local area to find out the reality of crime there for the community; â€¢ local victim surveys e.g. feminist â€“ research â€“ focused on a local area to uncover the dark figure of invisible crime; there is a particular focus on sexual assault and domestic abuse; â€¢ other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two). 4 0495/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2020 Page 19 of 33 Question Answer Marks

3(c) Explain how crime can be linked to new technologies. Candidates should show an understanding of how crime can be linked to new technologies. This is likely to be linked to the internet/social media and may refer to specific examples. Possible answers: â€¢ cybercrime â€“ crime involving the use of new technologies such as computers; â€¢ internet crime â€“ crime involving the use of the internet such as paeodophile rings, catfishing etc; â€¢ spreading computer viruses and malware through the internet; â€¢ increased possibilities for fraud and identity theft due to the internet e.g. phishing; â€¢ internet scams targeting individual users; â€¢ websites with obscene or offensive content e.g. the dark web; â€¢ harassment by cyberstalking or cyberbullying or trolling via social media; â€¢ trade in illegal goods e.g. drugs (internet makes it easier for the buyer and seller to contact each other); â€¢ cyberterrorism â€“ using the internet for widespread, deliberate attacks on computer networks; â€¢ organised global crime â€“ human trafficking e.g. in child labour, prostitution, slavery via the internet; â€¢ other reasonable response. Band 0 [0 marks] No creditworthy response. Band 1 [1â€“3 marks] Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of how crime can be linked to new technologies and may talk about e.g. â€˜ the internet has caused a lot of new crimes to appear such as identity theftâ€™ . Responses may be short and un/under developed. At the bottom of the band (1 mark), expect one weak point. Higher

question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜fertility rateâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two types of conjugal roles. [4] (b) Explain how families changed during industrialisation. [6] (c) Explain why so many alternatives to the family have developed in modern industrial societies. [8] (d) To what extent are feminist views of the family correct? [15] (e) Section B: Education There are many different types of schools students can attend today, such as private and state schools. Experiences of education and educational achievement vary greatly because of this.
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) What is meant by the term â€˜fertility rateâ€™? One mark for partial definition, e.g. number of births. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. the number of live births per 1000 women of child-bearing age in the population. 2

1(b) Describe two types of conjugal roles. Possible answers: â€¢ traditional conjugal roles â€“ segregated roles assumed to be normal and natural in the traditional nuclear family; â€¢ segregated conjugal roles â€“ the husband and wife perform very different family roles, e.g. female housewife and male breadwinner; â€¢ joint conjugal roles â€“ husband and wife carry out many tasks and activities together so there is no clear division between them (integrated conjugal roles); â€¢ maternal roles â€“ the woman is presumed to be the best suited to the nurturing, caring mother role (expressive role); â€¢ paternal roles â€“ man as family disciplinarian and authority figure, playing the breadwinner role (instrumental role); â€¢ new maternal role â€“ woman as breadwinner, single mother etc.; â€¢ new paternal role â€“ househusband, new man etc.; â€¢ reversed conjugal roles â€“ male househusband, female breadwinner; â€¢ other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 Candidates will describe two different types of conjugal roles. Conjugal roles = the roles taken by husband and wife within the family based on the domestic division of labour. Only roles of husband and/or wife are creditable â€“ roles of children, grandparents, etc. are not. 0495/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2020 Page 5 of 37 Question Answer Marks Guidance

1(c) Explain how families changed during industrialisation. Possible answers: â€¢ extended families were seen to fit the needs of pre-industrial societies whereas nuclear families were a better fit during industrialisation Parsons, the â€˜fit thesisâ€™; â€¢ in industrialisation institutions other than the family emerged who would take over many of the functions previously performed by families, e.g. healthcare and childcare. This meant the extended family was no longer needed; â€¢ industrial economies required workers who would move to wherever the work was available â€“ they needed to be geographically mobile. This was much easier for a small nuclear family than a large extended family; â€¢ grandparents â€“ family networks and wider kin may be involved in supporting the family in terms of childcare due to the rising trend for dual-worker families; â€¢ urbanisation accompanied industrialisation and saw a large movement of families out of rural areas to the cities. This corresponded with the decline in agricultural work; â€¢ industrialisation changed family roles, e.g. it created the segregated roles of the breadwinner and the housewife, childhood through children going to school; â€¢ dual worker families â€“ this trend has occurred because of rising living costs in the city and has resulted in a more flexible family structure,

[Turn over 06_0495_11_2021_1.2 *7485089898* Section A: Theory and methods Answer Question Source A In the 1990s sociological research suggested that the internet has a negative effect on individuals. In 2002 this view was challenged.The researchers Shaw and Gant investigated whether using the internet can have a positive effect on health and well-being. The sample for the study was composed of 40 students, 12 males and 28 females, in a university in North Carolina, USA. All participants volunteered for the study and their identities were kept anonymous. The experiment was then conducted over eight weeks. The participants had five internet chat sessions with another participant in the study, but never actually met each other in real life. Each pair of participants were given topics and questions to discuss online. At different stages in the study, each participant also met with the researcher who gave them a questionnaire to fill in. The questionnaire asked about their feelings about depression, loneliness, self-esteem and social support. The same questionnaire was used each time participants met again with the researcher and any changes in participantsâ€™ answers over time were noted. The study found that internet use significantly decreases feelings of loneliness and depression and significantly increases feelings of social support and self-esteem. Adapted from: Shaw and Gant, â€˜In Defense of the Internet: The Relationship between Internet Communication and Depression, Loneliness, Self-Esteem, and Perceived Social Supportâ€™. From the evidence in Source A identify two research methods that were used in the study. [2] (a) Identify two examples of secondary data that could be used to research the ways young people use the internet. [2] (b) Using information from Source A, describe two reasons why the research may not represent the experiences of all internet users. [4] (c) Describe two strengths of using content analysis as a research method. [4] (d) Describe two strengths and two limitations of semi-structured interviews. [8] (e) Explain why sociologists might use closed questions in sociological research. [10] (f) To what extent is a micro approach better than a macro approach when researching society? [15] (g) 06_0495_11_2021_1.2 2 Answer either Question 2 or Question

One mark for each reason correctly identified from the source (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each reason that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4

Marxists argue that the upper class have more power and 3 greater economic rewards than others. 3(b) Describe two examples of a gendered division of labour. Possible answers: â€¢ women do all or the vast majority of the housework â€“ for example cooking and cleaning whilst men do little of this; â€¢ women are expected to be the caretakers of children â€“ socialising and nurturing children into young adults whereas men take a secondary role, perhaps intervening when firm social control is needed; â€¢ segregated conjugal roles â€“ men expected to be the breadwinner and to go out to work whilst women have been expected to perform the housewife role; â€¢ horizontal segregation â€“ in the workplace women have been expected to perform roles in keeping with their gender e.g. domestic work, caring and clerical whilst men have been expected to have more technologically complex jobs like engineer and builder; â€¢ vertical segregation â€“ in the workplace women have traditionally been located in lower status roles within organisations with limited access to higher status and power, for example as secretaries whilst men have had more access to higher status roles such as being a supervisor, manager or the boss; â€¢ any other reasonable response. [Turn over 06_0495_12_2021_1.20 *8871552198* Section A: Theory and methods Answer Question In the Islington Crime Survey (1986) Jones, Lea and Young researched crime in an area of London called Islington. The surveyâ€™s aims were to find out the types and the frequency of crime in the area. The researchers hoped that this would provide more accurate data about crime in Islington than the official police records. The survey involved a random sample of over 2000 households from all parts of Islington. The response rate was approximately 75%. The researchers conducted structured interviews asking people about crimes they or their family had been a victim of during the last year. Respondents were asked about those who committed offences against them and whether they thought police were effective in tackling crime. Key findings from the study included: â€¢ the burglary rate in Islington was five times higher than the national average â€¢ a third of all households had experienced serious crime in the last twelve months (e.g. robbery, racist attacks and domestic violence) â€¢ over half of women avoided going out after dark due to fear of crime â€¢ more than 60% of respondents thought police were ineffective in dealing with serious crimes â€¢ only half of the crimes experienced by respondents were reported to the police, and only 3.8% of the crimes were detected by the police. Adapted from: https://islingtoncrimesurvey Source A From Source A identify two aims of the Islington Crime Survey. [2] (a) Identify two sampling methods, apart from random sampling. [2] (b) Using information from Source A, describe two problems with the validity of the data in the Islington Crime Survey. [4] (c) Describe two strengths of historical documents. [4] (d) Describe two strengths and two limitations of random sampling. [8] (e) Explain why sociologists might use covert participant observation in sociological research. [10] (f) To what extent are statistics useful in sociological research? [15] (g) 06_0495_12_2021_1.20 2 Answer either Question 2 or Question

1(b) Identify two sampling methods, apart from random sampling. 1(c) Using information from Source A, describe two problems with the validity of the data in the Islington Crime Survey. Possible answers: â€¢ the source is adapted and therefore there may have been changes made, data may have been missed out etc. which may mean it is not fully accurate, hence lowering the validity; â€¢ the interviews were structured , meaning that many closed questions are likely to be used. This means that little qualitative data would be gained about, for example, what people really thought and felt about crime in their area; â€¢ respondents were asked about crimes during the last year , they may not remember accurately affecting the validity of the data; â€¢ the survey is asking about peopleâ€™s experiences of crime â€“ some exaggerate, thus lowering validity; â€¢ this is a sensitive topic and so some people may not want to tell the truth, so lie and lowering validity; â€¢ the survey was taken in 1986 and thus may not be a true reflection of victimization in Islington today; â€¢ because a researcher is present some may respond with social desirability and only tell the researcher what they think the researcher wants to hear, thus not allowing the research to get an accurate picture; â€¢ there may be researcher bias in terms of the questions asked or the way in which the data is interpreted which will lower the validity of the data overall; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each problem correctly identified (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ) 4 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 7 of 22 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Describe two ways the media socialises individuals. Possible answers: â€¢ the behaviour and appearance of role-models in the media are imitated by many, especially the young; â€¢ individuals are manipulated by techniques used in the media such as advertising which may create â€˜false needsâ€™; â€¢ representations of women and other groups in popular media are influential in establishing social expectations for various forms of social identity such as gender, age etc; â€¢ the worldview reflected in global media is often a westernised or Americanised one which some may believe is leading to a global culture where individuals share common norms and values; â€¢ the media socially controls individuals by showing the negative consequences of not conforming to social norms and values e.g. labelling, â€˜naming and shamingâ€™ etc.; â€¢ the media uses praise and positive comments to show approval and reward conformity to social norms and values; â€¢ social media â€˜likesâ€™ can be seen as a reward for and expression of social approval for conformity; â€¢ cyberbullying and negative emojis online can be seen as expressions of disapproval and an attempt to negatively sanction an individual in order to bring them back to conformity with expected norms; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each way correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each way developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 13 of 22 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how values are a social construction. Possible responses: â€¢ values vary from society to society e.g. the acquisition of money brings status in some cultures whereas in others physical bravery or helping others may been seen as more important; â€¢ values are created in and by society rather than being naturally occurring e.g. we are taught values by parents in primary socialisation; â€¢ values vary between age groups e.g. the young may value appearance, popularity and peer approval more than older age groups; â€¢ values differ according to social class e.g. the working classes may place more importance on immediate gratification whereas the middle classes may think deferred gratification is crucial to future success; â€¢ values differ according to ethnicity and religion e.g. in many Asian and African cultures the extended family is still highly valued whereas it is not seen as important in more secularised western cultures; â€¢ values differ across time e.g. marriages in medieval England were often arranged for the purposes of social mobility and the maintenance of privilege whereas now love and physical attraction are valued more highly as a basis for marriage; â€¢ values are relative to time, place and culture, rather t

subject: Sociology
question_text: Section B: Culture, identity and socialisation Most sociologists agree values are a social construction. They also believe that people acquire their gender, class, ethnic and age identity through the agencies of socialisation and social control.
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) What is meant by the term â€˜meritocracyâ€™? Acceptable answer: â€¢ a society in which individuals achieve the level that their talents and abilities deserve. One mark for a partial definition e.g. move up the social ladder. Two marks for clear definition e.g. a society in which most individuals earn or achieve their status and rewards. 2

3(b) Describe two aspects of the dependency culture. Possible responses: â€¢ it arises when poor people live solely on benefits and become so used to them that they have no desire to work; â€¢ dependency culture is linked with fatalism and the idea that an individual is destined or has no power to lift themselves out of poverty; â€¢ a dependency culture is sometimes linked with negative social values like laziness and indolence which keeps people needing the help of the state or others for their welfare; â€¢ New Right thinkers argue that the welfare state is linked to a dependency culture as overgenerous welfare benefits encourage people to stay on them rather than find employment; â€¢ some might link dependency culture with life expectancy and the idea that old people become totally reliant on the younger generation within their family for help and support; â€¢ some argue that a dependency culture inevitably links to the cycle of poverty in which the values of dependency are passed down to succeeding generations who similarly cannot escape the poverty of their parents; â€¢ any other reasonable answer. One mark for each aspect correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each aspect developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 19 of 22 Question Answer Marks

3(c) Explain how the roles of women have changed in modern industrial societies. Possible responses: â€¢ due to changes in norms and laws womenâ€™s role in the home has changed dramatically with many women moving away from the traditional housewife role and into the workplace sometimes as breadwinners for their families; â€¢ in terms of their gender role women used to be considered weak and passive but now women have become more confident and empowered due to feminism; â€¢ feminists may argue that due to paid work many women now have a dual or triple burden and so their role within the home has now become more onerous; â€¢ changing divorce laws have now freed women from bad marriages and allowed them to enter the workplace and become independent/ breadwinners; â€¢ in the workplace there are now many successful role models for women to break through the glass ceiling e.g. Theresa May and Hilary Clinton in politics; â€¢ some, like Wilmott and Young, argue that the traditional gendered division of labour has now receded to be replaced by a more equal and shared division of household chores; â€¢ laws such as the Equal pay Act (1970) and the Sex Discrimination Act (1975) increased equality at work and paved the way for women to achieve higher pay and status in the workplace; â€¢ the roles of women in the media have chang

Possible answers: â€¢ women do all or the vast majority of the housework â€“ for example cooking and cleaning whilst men do little of this â€¢ women are expected to be the caretakers of children â€“ socialising and nurturing children into young adults whereas men take a secondary role, perhaps intervening when firm social control is needed â€¢ segregated conjugal roles â€“ men expected to be the breadwinner and to go out to work whilst women have been expected to perform the housewife role â€¢ horizontal segregation â€“ in the workplace women have been expected to perform roles in keeping with their gender, e.g. domestic work, caring and clerical whilst men have been expected to have more technologically complex jobs like engineer and builder â€¢ vertical segregation â€“ in the workplace women have traditionally been located in lower status roles within organisations with limited access to higher status and power, for example as secretaries whilst men have had more access to higher status roles such as being a supervisor, manager or the boss â€¢ any other reasonable response One mark for each example identified (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each example described (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2021 Page 16 of 20 Question Answer Marks

3(c) Explain how privileged groups maintain their power and status in society. Possible answers: â€¢ the rich â€“ Marxists argue that the bourgeoisie maintain their power and status by ideologies transmitted via education, religion and the media which justify and reinforce social hierarchy â€¢ Marxists argue that the bourgeoisie can ultimately rely on the forces of formal social control, such as the police and armed forces, to support the status quo in times of disorder â€¢ feminists argue that men maintain their power and status via sexism which acts as a barrier to female empowerment in all areas of society â€¢ radical feminists argue that some men use coercion or the threat of force or violence to keep women in a subordinate position in the family â€¢ ethnic minority groups are kept in a subordinate position to the ethnic majority through negative stereotyping and scapegoating, ensuring that such groups are viewed negatively and discriminated against in all areas of society â€¢ globally the most powerful developed countries protect their material advantages by exploiting the labour and resources of poor and developing countries â€¢ disabled people are often the subject of negative stereotyping and discrimination in the workplace and this can result in individuals not feeling able to

question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜value consensusâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two examples of youth sub-cultures. [4] (b) Explain how children are socialised by agencies of secondary socialisation. [6] (c) Explain why modern industrial societies are child-centred. [8] (d) To what extent has globalisation created a global culture? [15] (e) Section C: Social inequality Sociologists study different types of society such as open, closed, traditional and industrial societies. One of the features of an open society is that social mobility between social classes is possible.
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) What is meant by the term â€˜value consensusâ€™? One mark for partial definition e.g. agree on things. Two marks for clear definition e.g. general agreement across a society on a set of values. 2

2(b) Describe two examples of youth sub-cultures. Possible responses: â€¢ mods â€“ rode scooters and wore Parker jackets; â€¢ rockers â€“ rode motorbikes and wore leather and denim; â€¢ teddy boys â€“ wore subverted Edwardian clothing; â€¢ McRobbieâ€™s bedroom culture â€“ characterised by inhabiting a gendered space in the bedroom; â€¢ goths â€“ heavy make-up with black clothing; â€¢ punks â€“ ripped clothes and spikey hair; â€¢ hippies â€“ a middle class youth sub-culture that used drugs to explore their inner feelings; â€¢ skinheads â€“ based on aggressive masculine behaviour and the wearing of big boots; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 11 of 19 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how children are socialised by agencies of secondary socialisation. Possible responses: â€¢ hidden curriculum â€“ schools have many rules to which pupils must conform e.g. respect for teachers; â€¢ sanctions â€“ institutions such as school offer positive or negative sanctions to socialise pupils into accepting norms and values, the police also may sanction children to control deviant behaviour; â€¢ rewards â€“ religious organisations offer the promise of an afterlife to those who adhere to their moral guidance; â€¢ peer pressure â€“ children may succumb to peer pressure in order to fit in with a peer group; â€¢ ostracism â€“ children who donâ€™t conform to the values of peer groups may sanction through ostracism and made to feel like outsiders; â€¢ role modelling â€“ children are faced with role models, whoâ€™s attributes they may wish to have, in various agencies of secondary socialisation such as schools or the media; â€¢ copying/imitation â€“ the media is a large part of childrenâ€™s life and being impressionable they are likely to copy behaviours they see e.g. Bandura Bobo doll experiment; â€¢ any other reasonable response. Band 0 [0 marks] No creditworthy response. Band 1 [1â€“3 marks] Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of the question. Responses may be short and un/underdeveloped. At the bottom of the band (1 mark), expect one weak point. Higher in the band (2â€“3 marks), candidates may offer more than one weak point, but there may be a tendency towards simplistic answers, engaging with sociological ideas without using sociological language. Band 2 [4â€“6 marks] Answers in this band will have clear and accurate explanation, showing good sociological knowledge and understanding. Sociological terms and concepts should be expected and explained. At the bottom of the band, the range of points covered may be narrow or lack detail. question_text: B Estimated age of respondent 16 or under 17â€“21 22â€“40 41â€“60 61 or over 1 2 3 4 The various methods used to persuade or force individuals to conform to societyâ€™s norms and values are known as social control. Social control is one way in which deviance can be avoided and social conformity maintained. (a) What is meant by the term â€˜social conformityâ€™? [2] (b) Describe two agencies of social control. [4] (c) Explain how informal agencies of social control use rewards and sanctions to ensure conformity. [6] (d) Explain why norms and values vary widely from one country to another. [8] (e) To what extent is role conflict more of a problem in modern industrial societies than in the past? [15] Section C: Social inequality
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) What is meant by the term â€˜social conformityâ€™? One mark for partial definition e.g. doing as expected Two marks for clear definition e.g. behaviour that matches that expected by the rest of the social group. 2

2(b) Describe two agencies of social control. Possible responses: â€¢ police e.g. the power of arrest deters people from deviant action; â€¢ courts/judiciary e.g. sentences such as imprisonment act as a deterrent to keep the majority in conformity; â€¢ penal system e.g. being locked up and having no freedom prevents criminals from committing deviant acts; â€¢ armed forces e.g. in situations such as riots the army may be brought onto the streets and the threat of coercion will likely prevent further unrest; â€¢ family e.g. manipulation into gender roles and the use of sanctions such as grounding keep children in line; â€¢ media e.g. show punishments that rule-breakers receive; â€¢ school/education e.g. use of detentions and the setting of rules in the hidden curriculum; â€¢ religion e.g. laws and rules are set out and rewards/punishments such as heaven and hell are given; â€¢ work e.g. promotion in the workplace encourages people to follow the norms and values of the company; â€¢ peer group e.g. ridicule from friends for deviating from expected norms; â€¢ government e.g. set laws and punishments; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each agency correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each agency that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 12 of 20 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how informal agencies of social control use rewards and sanctions to ensure conformity . Possible answers: â€¢ school e.g. detentions may deter further rule-breaking whereas praise from the teacher will encourage further conformity; â€¢ family e.g. social media bans or being grounded are likely to produce better behaviour, as are rewards such as pocket money and praise; â€¢ peer group e.g. being ridiculed or ostracised by friends is painful and is likely to bring behaviour back into line whilst praise from peers will likely foster conformity with group norms; â€¢ workplace e.g. written warnings or demotion is likely to change someoneâ€™s behaviour as these are serious consequences and pay rises and awards will foster further conformity; â€¢ religion e.g. the threat of hell or the promise of heaven will likely keep believers in line with official teachings; â€¢ any other reasonable response. subject: Sociology
question_text: Marriage takes different forms in different cultures and societies. In modern industrial societies rates of divorce are high. This may be linked to the domestic division of labour and feminism. (a) What is meant by the term â€˜domestic division of labourâ€™? [2] (b) Describe two ways the family can be dysfunctional. [4] (c) Explain how marriages vary in different cultures. [6] (d) Explain why there has been a rise in divorce rates in modern industrial societies. [8] (e) To what extent are family roles today equal? [15] Section B: Education
mark_scheme_text:
1(a) What is meant by the term â€˜domestic division of labourâ€™? The way in which tasks in the home, such as cooking, cleaning, household repairs and childcare, are divided between the man and the woman. One mark for partial definition, e.g. woman does the cleaning. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. males and females in the household take different roles i.e. men do the DIY and women do the childcare. 2

1(b) Describe two ways the family can be dysfunctional. Possible answers: â€¢ domestic violence spouse to spouse; â€¢ elder abuse (extended families); â€¢ gender socialisation restricting females; â€¢ marital breakdown and divorce: effect on spouses; â€¢ marital breakdown and divorce: effect on children; â€¢ New Right view: cultural deprivation e.g. underclass theories; â€¢ single parent families as dysfunctional â€“ lack of adequate socialisation/lack of male role model etc; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 6 of 30 Question Answer Marks

1(c) Explain how marriages vary in different cultures. Possible answers: â€¢ monogamy â€“ being married to one person at a time; â€¢ polygamy â€“ being married to more than one person at the same time; â€¢ serial monogamy â€“ when someone has more than one marriage partner during their life, but only one at a time; â€¢ polygyny â€“ when a man has more than one wife at the same time; â€¢ polyandry â€“ when a woman has more than one husband at the same time; â€¢ empty shell marriage â€“ a married couple continue to live together but without love or affection; â€¢ arranged marriage â€“ a marriage which is arranged by the parents of the marriage partners/a union between two families; â€¢ forced marriage â€“ a marriage in which one or more of the partners is married without his/her consent; â€¢ variations in marriage ceremonies and venues e.g. Christians marrying in churches, secular societies using non-religious venues etc; â€¢ any other reasonable response. Band 0 [0 marks] No creditworthy response. Band 1 [1â€“3 marks] Answers in this band will show only a limited awareness of the idea that marriages are different in different cultures and may talk about e.g. â€˜ marriages being different for different peopleâ€™ . Responses may be short and un/under developed. At the bottom of the band (1 mark), expect one weak point. Higher in the band (2â€“3 marks), candidates may offer more than one weak point, but there may be a tendency towards simplistic answers, engaging with sociological ideas without using sociological language. Band 2 [4â€“6 marks] A clear and accurate explanation, showing good sociological knowledge and understanding of the idea that marriages are different in different cultures. subject: Sociology
question_text: According to the official crime statistics, some social groups are more likely than others to be convicted of crime. Some sociologists question how effective punishments actually are at deterring people from committing crime. This is particularly true of young people who are part of youth culture. (a) What is meant by the term â€˜youth cultureâ€™? [2] (b) Describe two reasons why someone might not report a crime to the police. [4] (c) Explain how self-report studies can be used to measure crime. [6] (d) Explain why some ethnic minority groups have higher official rates of crime than other ethnic groups. [8] (e) To what extent do punishments for committing crimes deter people from criminal behaviour? [15] Section D: Media
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) What is meant by the term â€˜youth cultureâ€™? The way of life (norms and values) of young people between childhood and adulthood. One mark for partial definition, e.g. way of life. Two marks for clear definition, e.g. norms and values associated with young people. 2

3(b) Describe two reasons why someone might not report a crime to the police. Possible answers: â€¢ embarrassment e.g. sexual crimes; â€¢ crime not perceived as criminal e.g. relating to health and safety legislation; â€¢ petty crimes â€“ seen to be a waste of time, nothing will be done; â€¢ not aware a crime has been committed e.g. child neglect â€“ the young person does not know that they have been a victim of crime â€¢ not able to report a crime e.g. small children that are victims; â€¢ fear of reprisals e.g. being scared of the criminal; â€¢ knowing the perpetrator and not wanting to get them into trouble e.g. crimes committed by family members/friends; â€¢ new crimes such as those to do with technology and the internet e.g. cybercrimes are so commonplace that they are just accepted as â€˜normalâ€™; â€¢ feeling you will not be listened to e.g. corrupt/untrustworthy police force; â€¢ acceptance of crime e.g. gang crime/criminal subcultures â€“ where criminal behaviour is a normal part of everyday life; â€¢ crimes the victim is unaware of e.g. they think they have lost something; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2021 Page 19 of 30 Question Answer Marks

[Turn over 06_0495_12_2023_1.9 *5130322510* Section A: Theory and methods Answer Question Zimbardo (1973) conducted an experiment on conformity to social roles. His aim was to investigate whether people would conform to the social roles of a prison guard or prisoner when they were placed in a pretend prison environment. Zimbardo had a sample of 21 male university students. The students volunteered in response to a newspaper advert. The students were then randomly selected to play the role of either a prisoner or a guard. Zimbardo wanted to make the experiment realistic so he turned part of a building into a pretend prison with cells. The students who played the role of prisoners were also arrested by real police, had their fingerprints taken and were given prison clothes to wear with chains around one ankle. The guards wore uniforms and carried handcuffs and a baton. Researchers told the guards to run the prison for two weeks without using physical violence. However, the guards became abusive when the prisoners began to complain. Five of the prisoners left the experiment early because they could not cope physically or mentally. For example, they were crying and experienced extreme anxiety. Zimbardo found that both the prisoners and guards soon identified with their social roles. He concluded that people quickly conform to social roles, even when the role goes against their moral beliefs. [Turn over 06_0495_13_2023_1.9 *8855459579* Section A: Theory and methods Answer Question A 55 year old teacher in a private school in Mumbai conducted some research with his students. The teacher used questionnaires to investigate how young people use social media. The teacher had never used social media. The reason for doing the research was that many staff in the school were concerned about the amount of time students spent looking at their phones, both in their lessons and their leisure time. The teacher gave out 20 questionnaires to boys who were in his sociology class. He waited with them whilst they completed the questionnaires in silence. The questionnaire used a mixture of closed and pre-coded questions. As an incentive the boys were told that they could leave class early for lunch once their questionnaire had been filled in. The teacherâ€™s findings were that the majority of the boys claimed to use social media only after school and for just 1-2 hours per day. The students claimed to use social media mainly to communicate with friends or family. However, many students also stated they used social media to look for resources to help in their studies. question_text: Section B: Culture, identity and socialisation Primary and secondary socialisation are both important processes. They help to influence the different aspects of social identity, such as gender identity. Sociologists study how the peer group 2 influences individuals during adolescence and also why the hidden curriculum is so effective in socialising the young. In addition, modern industrial societies use formal agents of social control to ensure conformity. What is meant by the term â€˜primary socialisationâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two ways formal agents of social control ensure conformity. [4] (b) Explain how the peer group influences young people during adolescence. [6] (c) Explain why the hidden curriculum is an effective way of socialising students. [8] (d) To what extent is gender the most important aspect of social identity? [15] (e) Section C: Social inequality Ageism and sexism still exist in many societies. Many sociologists argue that the unequal distribution of wealth is the main form of social inequality. Governments have tried to reduce social inequality 3 in various ways. However, it is clear that the causes of inequality are complex. Some people blame the government whilst other people think that a culture of poverty exists. What is meant by the term â€˜ageismâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two ways that governments try to reduce inequality in societies. [4] (b) Explain how some social groups experience prejudice in society. [6] (c) Explain why some sociologists argue that a culture of poverty exists. [8] (d) To what extent is the unequal distribution of wealth the main form of social inequality? 3(a) What is meant by the term â€˜ageismâ€™? One mark for partial definition e.g. young people being unemployed. Two marks for clear definition e.g. prejudice or discrimination against someone based on their age. 2

3(b) Describe two ways that governments try to reduce inequality in societies. Possible answers: ï‚· through education e.g. giving free education to all so that individuals begin life on broadly the same terms and have the same chances for social mobility; ï‚· through the welfare state e.g. benefit systems which ensure that the basic needs of the poor and unemployed are met by giving them money to live; ï‚· through free healthcare e.g. the NHS where treatment is free at the point of delivery and people are treated according to need not their capacity to pay; ï‚· the use of taxation e.g. progressive taxation which takes a higher rate from the rich âˆ’ money is then redistributed to help the poor; ï‚· through legislation e.g. the Equal Pay Act which ensures that individuals have the right to the same pay for the same work regardless of gender, ethnicity etc.; ï‚· governments often provide scholarships and bursaries to disadvantaged young people which may help to reduce inequalities in education; ï‚· any other reasonable response. question_text: Youth sub-cultures may help to form the social identity of adolescents through their social interactions. What is meant by the term â€˜social interactionâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two roles that adolescents may have in modern industrial societies. [4] (b) Explain how value consensus is maintained. [6] (c) Explain why gender is an important influence on identity. [8] (d) To what extent are Marxist explanations of youth sub-cultures the most useful? 2(b) Describe two roles that adolescents may have in modern industrial societies. Possible answers: â€¢ son or daughter â€“ role defined by relationship to and expectations of parent(s); â€¢ student â€“ role defined by expected behaviours in school such as being punctual or completing homework; â€¢ employee â€“ many adolescents have part - time paid jobs outside of school; â€¢ friend â€“ adolescents will follow the norms of their peer/friendship group; â€¢ member of a sub - culture â€“ adolescents join sub - cultures such as punks or Goths where they play a deviant role; â€¢ any other reasonable response. 2(c) Explain how value consensus is maintained. Possible answers: â€¢ functionalist theory (organic analogy) â€“ social institutions work together to maintain social order and avoid chaos; â€¢ processes of socialisation â€“ learning common norms and values especially during secondary socialisation means that people have common beliefs and standards; â€¢ hidden curriculum in school (e.g. hierarchy/respect for authority) is taught through the use of rewards and sanctions leading to social consensus and social order; â€¢ through media representations which reinforce societal values and norms of behaviour; â€¢ the use of peer pressure in enforcing values (of society or the peer group) via ostracism for example; â€¢ coercion e.g. those who donâ€™t conform to the consensus face serious sanctions e.g. prison and this sends a message/deters others from straying from the value consensus; â€¢ the workplace reinforces expected values through rewards and sanctions e.g. promotion or dismissal; â€¢ in the family via primary socialisation e.g. values of respect for elders are encouraged and reinforced; â€¢ ideological state apparatus â€“ the Marxist view of agencies of socialisation as brainwashing people into a common set of values which reinforces the inequalities at the heart of capitalism; â€¢ any other reasonable response. 4(c) Explain how new media and traditional media are different. Possible answers: â€¢ traditional media broadcasts to a mass target audience whereas new media allows for narrow - casting and a more specialist / niche target audience; â€¢ interactivity â€“ new media is typically more interactive for the consumer than traditional media which is one - way communication; â€¢ social media â€“ this is a big part of new media, allowing for connections and networking; it is not a part of traditional media; â€¢ top - down media â€“ represents the hierarchy in traditional media where the audience are often seen as passive consumers; with new media this hierarchy tends to be more democratic; â€¢ traditional media is typically conservative and follows the status quo (Marxism) whereas new media can be more alternative and progressive in its content and style; â€¢ active audience â€“ the audience are active contributors to new media (e.g. citizen journalism / user generated content) whereas they are merely consumers in traditional media; â€¢ internet â€“ new media uses the internet and so allows for globalisation whereas traditional media is typically more national / regional in focus; â€¢ platforms â€“ new media use a wide range of different platforms for their content which can be accessed on a wide range of devices â€“ traditional media has less flexibility; â€¢ any other reasonable response. Possible answers: â€¢ financially â€“ education can lead to earning more money and achieving more wealth; â€¢ status â€“ a good education can allow people to climb the social hierarchy and so achieve higher status and more self - esteem and self - confidence; â€¢ employment â€“ education opens the door to many more types of employment giving the individual choice and freedom; â€¢ social networks â€“ as a person goes higher in the education system they expand their range of social networks meaning they have more potentially useful social contacts later in life; â€¢ cultural capital (Bourdieu) â€“ the more educated a person is, the more cultural capital they have and as this is highly valued by society it is likely to improve a personâ€™s life chances; â€¢ housing â€“ if education le ad s to more economic capital then this allows individuals to get onto the housing ladder / live in better areas/conditions; â€¢ travel â€“ education often offers opportunities for extended travel â€“ school trips, work experience placements, a year abroad , etc. â€“ these all offer better opportunities to the person; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 12 of 28 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how formal education is different to informal education. Possible answers: â€¢ formal education is taught in schools/educational establishments â€“ informal education can happen in communities, at home, through daily interactions , etc. ; â€¢ formal education is based on a set, agreed syllabus of content that has to be taught, informal education is flexible and is not prescribed; â€¢ formal education is assessed through examinations or controlled assessment or coursework based on universally agreed standards â€“ informal education is not; â€¢ informal education may be referred to as the hidden curriculum, what children learn in schools that is not part of the formal lesson content (formal education) e.g. punctuality, respect, manners , etc. â€“ formal education is explicit and structured; â€¢ informal education may be more about socialisation and culture, whereas formal education is about prescribed content learning; â€¢ any other reasonable response. 2(b) Describe two examples of formal education. Possible answers: ï‚· setting â€“ formal education takes place in classrooms inside schools (institutions or schools created for the purpose of learning); ï‚· content â€“ learners are taught set, agreed content from a syllabus; ï‚· roles â€“ there are clearly defined roles of teachers and learners within formal education; ï‚· assessment â€“ standardised testing is used to judge candidate performance e.g. national examinations; ï‚· any other reasonable response. 2(c) Explain how comprehensive schools function. Possible answers: ï‚· non-selective â€“ entry to a comprehensive school is open to all children of that secondary school age, regardless of ability; ï‚· local area â€“ comprehensive schools were said to encourage social mixing as all children in a local area would be educated together resulting in a strong, cohesive community spirit; ï‚· free â€“ comprehensive schools are paid for and run by the government meaning no one is prevented from attending because of financial hardship; ï‚· life chances â€“ comprehensive schools offered all students the chance to succeed in their qualifications and so improve their life chances through upward social mobility â€“ very few students leave comprehensive schools without any qualifications; ï‚· replaced tripartite system â€“ introduced in 1960â€™s in the UK and replaced the â€˜three school systemâ€™ based on ability and testing that was thought to label children unfairly aged 11; ï‚· meritocratic â€“ comprehensive schools are thought to be a fairer system of schooling where all children are given equal opportunities and chances of success through studying the same subjects and curriculum, judging everyone on an equal footing via universal standards; ï‚· working class children â€“ comprehensive schools are thought to particularly benefit working class children who are the least likely to be able to get into grammar schools due to material and cultural deprivation; ï‚· any other reasonable response. 3(a) What is meant by the term â€˜cybercrimeâ€™? One mark for partial definition e.g. online fraud. Two marks for clear definition e.g. crimes involving the use of new technologies like the internet. 2

3(b) Describe two features of the dark figure of crime. Possible answers: ï‚· unreported crimes â€“ not all crimes are reported to the police by the public due to fear of reprisals by the perpetrators e.g. in communities where crime is rife; ï‚· unreported crimes â€“ not all crimes are reported to the police by the public because the criminals are friends or family members and this leads to conflicted loyalties; ï‚· unrecorded crimes â€“ the police do not record all crimes that are reported to them due to police discretion and pressure on the police to â€˜clear upâ€™ crimes (coughing and cuffing); ï‚· sexual crimes â€“ these are notoriously underreported due to victim embarrassment and a culture whereby sexual crimes rarely result in a conviction; ï‚· recognition of a crime taking place â€“ often people are unsure if they have actually been the victim of crime e.g. online crimes/scams therefore they are never reported; ï‚· any other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 20 of 32 Question Answer Marks

3(c) Explain how the findings from victim surveys may not be accurate. Possible answers: ï‚· memory â€“ victim surveys rely on respondentâ€™s memories which may be faulty â€“ this reduces their validity; ï‚· cannot cover all types of crimes â€“ they do not usually cover victimless crimes e.g. drug use, crimes against children and crimes against organisations. Therefore, they only give a partial picture of the victims of crimes; ï‚· national picture â€“ most victim surveys are completed using respondents from all over a nation â€“ this does not show the impact or seriousness of crime in localised areas of a country; ï‚· quantitative data â€“ the most common type of victim surveys are large scale and produce quantitative data e.g. the CSEW or BCS â€“ these cannot explore the responses or feelings of victims to crime; ï‚· local victim surveys â€“ these are carried out in a specific local area and therefore cannot be representative of the wider population so making generalisations impossible e.g. the Islington survey; ï‚· feminist victim surveys â€“ e.g. Walklate â€“ these can be criticised for focusing only on sexual crimes and not considering the impact of other kinds of crimes on victims; ï‚· underreported crimes â€“ some types of crimes e.g. sexual crimes, are still thought to be underreported in victim surveys due to people not wanting to talk to strangers about such an intimate, personal experience; ï‚· any other reasonable response. 1(b) Describe two examples of child-centredness. Possible answers: ï‚· legislation e.g. UN Rights of the Child - gives power to children e.g. in divorce cases; ï‚· corporal punishment â€“ now banned in schools in most countries; ï‚· parental smacking â€“ this is viewed as unacceptable/illegal in many societies; ï‚· period of innocence â€“ childhood is seen in many societies as a time when children should not have to work or experience other aspects of adult life; ï‚· extension of education â€“ extending compulsory schooling maintains the status of â€˜childâ€™ for longer as education is considered very important; ï‚· children as consumers â€“ industries reinforce the special status of â€˜childhoodâ€™ through the production of specialised goods for them e.g. TV channels/shows, toys, clothes etc.; ï‚· spending on children â€“ this has increased enormously with phenomenon like â€˜pester powerâ€™ encouraging adults to purchase goods for children to consume; ï‚· leisure time â€“ family time and activities often centre around the needs and wants of the child rather than the adult; ï‚· other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to a maximum of two ). 1(c) Explain how family life is globally diverse. Possible answers: ï‚· type of family â€“ different countries/cultures have different types of family, all of which can be linked to differences in family life e.g. living in an extended family is very different to living in a step-family; ï‚· ethnicity â€“ ethnic groups are sometimes associated with particular kinds of family life e.g. Afro-Caribbean families may be matrifocal, South Asian families may be extended and larger etc.; ï‚· marriages â€“ these can be different globally, leading to differences in family life e.g. South Asian families may encourage arranged marriages, some Islamic and tribal cultures allow polygamy etc.; ï‚· gender roles â€“ these may be segregated in some more traditional societies whereas in other societies e.g. in the UK, they are more likely to be joint showing the diversity of family life; ï‚· norms and values â€“ different families across the globe will socialise their children into different norms and values, depending upon their culture and/or religion e.g. high birth rates in many Eastern European countries, a declining birth rate in China; ï‚· social class/money â€“ this can affect family life e.g. family experiences, trips out, cultural capital, values, expectations, material items, space at home etc. and this obviously differs globally; ï‚· any other reasonable response. Possible answers: ï‚· meritocracy â€“ you will be setted/get results based on your effort and ability; ï‚· national curriculum â€“ all students must follow the same curriculum for core subjects which is fair and equal; ï‚· free education â€“ in many societies education is free to all, regardless of background e.g. state schools; ï‚· subject choice â€“ all students in a school have the same choice of subjects, regardless of social factors; ï‚· social mobility â€“ upward and downward mobility is a feature of life for everybody, based on educational success; ï‚· comprehensive schools â€“ these were set up to offer equality of education to all students in a local area; ï‚· compensatory education â€“ many schools use schemes such as bursaries and/or scholarships in order to ensure all social groups get equal opportunities for success (positive discrimination); ï‚· co-ed teaching/schools â€“ offering girls and boys the same educational opportunities and curriculum; ï‚· any other reasonable response. 1(b) Describe two positive features of extended families. Possible answers: ï‚· childcare â€“ vertically extended families can help working parents out with free childcare; ï‚· emotional support/advice â€“ elders can act as a source of support and advice to their children; this is thought to be particularly relevant for females in extended families; ï‚· financial assistance â€“ extended families often help other family members out with financial aid, loans, monetary gifts etc.; ï‚· greater tolerance â€“ living with different generations can promote a sense of understanding and acceptance between generations with often quite different norms and values; ï‚· elder care â€“ children can be part of the care process for the elderly, helping to keep older people at home surrounded by loving family; ï‚· any other reasonable response. 1(c) Explain how childrenâ€™s roles in the family have changed over time. Possible answers: ï‚· legislation â€“ childrenâ€™s lives are now regulated by the law which has changed their roles in the family e.g. they must now go to school, laws about what age they can work etc â€“ childhood is a period of innocence; ï‚· paid work â€“ in the Middle Ages children were â€˜mini adultsâ€™, working and being treated the same as adults; today their role in the family is now marked as a different life-stage to an adult (Aries); ï‚· social control â€“ children are more controlled today by parents and other institutions than they were in the past e.g. surveillance, schools, apps, rules, trackers etc.; ï‚· childrenâ€™s power â€“ some law changes give children more power than before e.g. UN Rights of the Child â€“ in relation to physical punishments, having a say in decisions that affect them (i.e. divorce and custody) and being listened to; ï‚· child-centred â€“ children are now often the central focus of family life with day-to-day activities and meals being centred around them. They are also specialised consumers with a clearly defined status; ï‚· boomerang family â€“ children may leave home to go to university or to live with a partner but then return to the parental home as adults at a later point because of contemporary difficulties with the increased cost of living; ï‚· pivot generation â€“ in many western countries due to the ageing population, it is now commonplace for adult children to face a dual burden â€“ having to care for both their young children and their parents; ï‚· any other reasonable response. 2(b) Describe two ways schools socially control students. Possible answers: ï‚· formal social control â€“ schools use formal school rules to control pupils and ensure they obey those in authority, e.g. uniform rules, behaviour policy, homework policy etc.; ï‚· informal social control â€“ flexible measures of social control are frequently used within schools to control students e.g. a stern look or a smile in order to encourage conformity; ï‚· negative sanctions â€“ punishments are used in schools to control pupils and deter them from further bad behaviour e.g. detentions, negative contact with home; ï‚· rewards (positive sanctions) â€“ praise and other rewards such as positive contact home/treat trips are used to promote and encourage good behaviour by school pupils (social conformity); ï‚· peer group â€“ peer groups can use peer pressure and role models in order to control the behaviour and attitudes of others in the peer group who are keen to belong e.g. pro and anti-school subcultures; ï‚· any other reasonable response. mark_scheme_text:
2(a) What is meant by the term â€˜diversityâ€™? One mark for a partial definition e.g. diversity means difference . Two marks for a clear definition e.g. the wide differences between/within human cultures or societies . 2

2(b) Describe two examples of peer pressure. Possible answers: â€¢ bullying others either physically or psychologically in order to get an individual to conform to the peer norms e.g. cyber bullying or trolling; â€¢ ostracism or the threat of exclusion from the peer group often brings conformity; â€¢ rejection by peers following deviant behaviour can have a powerful emotional effect on individuals and function as a spur to change behaviour to bring it back into line with peer norms; â€¢ acceptance into a group expresses approval for conformity to group norms and values e.g. via a rite of passage; â€¢ giving praise and compliments as rewards for conformist behaviour will encourage its continuance; â€¢ the achievement of higher status and credibility within the peer group for role modelling conformity to group norms and values; â€¢ the extension of friendship to those deemed to fit in with group norms encourages an emotional bond that is cemented by continued conformity; â€¢ the pressure an individual applies to themselves due to the desire to fit in with their peers e.g. wearing a clothing brand in order to fit in; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each correctly identified example (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each example that is appropriately developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495 / 12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 12 of 21 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Explain how primary socialisation affects childrenâ€™s gender roles. question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜peer pressureâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two ways children learn social expectations through imitation. [4] (b) Explain how role conflict occurs. [6] (c) Explain why some youth sub-cultures are non-conformist. [8] (d) To what extent is primary socialisation the most influential type of socialisation? [15] (e) Section C: Social inequality Prejudice and poverty are experienced throughout the world. However, sociologists have different ways of measuring poverty. Sociologists also research why the elderly may lack status and whether gender affects an individualâ€™s life chances.
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) What is meant by the term â€˜peer pressureâ€™? One mark for partial definition e.g. people making you do things. Two marks for clear definition e.g. the influence or power people with the same status as you have to make you conform. 2

2(b) Describe two ways children learn social expectations through imitation. Possible answers: â€¢ children see their parents performing roles at home and imitate the behaviour they see e.g. picking up rubbish and placing it in the bin; â€¢ children form groups at school and nursery and imitate the expected behaviour shown by peers e.g. sharing toys and taking turns; â€¢ children see expected behaviour via the media and in turn imitate the behaviour they see e.g. in television dramas; â€¢ children are shown how to conduct activities safely through role modelling e.g. children are taught to imitate adults when they learn to cross the road; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each way correctly identified (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each way that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4

2(c) Explain how role conflict occurs. Possible response s: â€¢ having friends at work can cause role conflict as your boss may need to tell you what to do at work but in a social situation the roles are more equal; â€¢ in a family business it is difficult to separate achieved employment roles from the ascribed family roles; â€¢ adults may have to switch from mother/father to spouse and to child within the extended family environment; â€¢ a parent could also be your teacher if you go to the school where your parent teaches and this will cause role conflict; â€¢ pressures to perform the work role often conflict with pressures of the parent role â€“ for example when a child cannot go to school parents are caught between conflicting roles; â€¢ role conflict between student and peer group member â€“ students may want to get on at school but fear losing credibility with their peer group and hence fall prey to negative peer pressure; â€¢ role conflict between the role of daughter/son and girlfriend/boyfriend â€“ young people can face competing demands on the emotional time and this may result in a distancing from the family members as more social time is spent with boy/girlfriends; â€¢ role conflict between sister/brother and friend â€“ older siblings may be expected to look after younger siblings when theyâ€™d rather be out with their friends and this may result in clashes with parents; â€¢ role conflict between work and domestic duty, e.g. not being able to keep on top of domestic duties and manage a paid job â€“ dual burden; â€¢ any other reasonable answer. 6 0495 / 13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 12 of 20 Question Answer Marks

question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜family functionsâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two demographic trends affecting the family. [4] (b) Explain how family life can vary cross-culturally. 1(b) Describe two demographic trends affecting the family. Possible answers: â€¢ birth rate â€“ in m odern i ndustrial s ocieties ( MISs) the birth rate is falling leading to smaller family sizes; â€¢ fertility rate â€“ women are having fewer children than in the past therefore children have fewer/no siblings; â€¢ beanpole family â€“ smaller family size means more families with only a few people in each generation are becoming typical; â€¢ DINK families â€“ the rise of childless families through choice e.g. career focused; â€¢ new baby boom â€“ immigration to MISs has led some to believe there is a baby boom, particularly amongst those from Eastern European origin; â€¢ births outside marriage â€“ these are increasing through decline of marriage and increase of cohabitation and single parent families; â€¢ death rate â€“ this has fallen leading to an ageing population in many MISs which can lead to the burden of care falling on family members; â€¢ child mortality rate â€“ has fallen dramatically as healthcare improves therefore less pressure on families to have lots of children ; â€¢ other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). 1(c) Explain how family life can vary cross - culturally. Possible answers: â€¢ family type â€“ nuclear families are common in many Western cultures whereas extended families dominate in South Asian cultures; â€¢ family size â€“ this differs cross - culturally, for example Western families are quite small, Chinese family size is legislated by state rule (currently 3) and Asian families may have more children; â€¢ family structure â€“ Western families are typically beanpole in structure whereas Asian families may extend vertically and/or horizontally; â€¢ same - sex families â€“ these have now become normalised in a number of countries, particularly in the West, whereas in many religious cultures these are not seen to be acceptable or are illegal; â€¢ gender roles â€“ cultural norms often determine family gender roles such as in many traditional cultures e.g. traveller culture, females are still expected to take responsibility for childcare and domestic work whereas in many more modern cultures roles are becoming more symmetrical; â€¢ communes/shared living arrangements â€“ families who live in communes have a very different experience of living in a family (shared parenting/non - familial living arrangements etc . ) than those living in a more traditional family structure; â€¢ divorce â€“ in some traditional/religious cultures divorce is illegal and therefore famil y life may be impacted by negative

subject: Sociology
question_text: What is meant by the term â€˜the pressâ€™? [2] (a) Describe two sociological criticisms of the media. [4] (b) Explain how people of different ages use the media. [6] (c) Explain why media representations are changing. [8] (d) To what extent is media censorship possible? [15] (e) 11_0495_21_2024_1.13
mark_scheme_text:
4(a) What is meant by the term â€˜the pressâ€™? One mark for partial definition e.g. journalists ; Two marks for clear definition e.g. newspaper and magazine publishing industry. 2

4(b) Describe two sociological criticisms of the media. Possible answers: â€¢ convey dominant ideology â€“ the media keep the population in a state of false consciousness because they are owned and controlled by the capitalist class who deliberately use them to keep power and prevent social change e.g. Miliband; â€¢ manipulation â€“ the media can manipulate people to believe certain things e.g. the UK media was said to have manipulated people to support the invasion of Iraq through scaremongering about the threat of invasion â€“ bias; â€¢ false needs â€“ the media creates false needs, making people be passive consumers who are willing to work hard in order to buy things they have been persuaded by the media that they need; â€¢ diversion â€“ the media diverts attention away from important social issues through feeding the audience a diet of trivia and ignoring radical or alternative ideas for change; â€¢ GUMG â€“ the media provides a distorted view of reality e.g. through dismissing and ridiculing the trade unions and supporting management/owners in strikes and disputes; â€¢ decision makers in the media â€“ tend to be white, male, middle aged and middle class therefore they support a narrow world view and ideology through their editing and journalistic decisions; â€¢ moral panics â€“ the media distorts reality and creates folk devils and moral panics in society e.g. Islamophobia , mods and rockers, football hooligans; â€¢ invisibility â€“ some social groups are under - represented in the media e.g. ethnic minorities, creating an ethnocentric/white - washed media industry; â€¢ male gaze â€“ Mulvey â€“ the media is said to sexualise and objectify females e.g. the cult of femininity; â€¢ media culture â€“ the media is now so prevalent in society that it reduces real human interaction e.g. texting not talking, dating apps, online gaming etc . ; â€¢ feminism â€“ females are negatively stereotyped in the media e.g. in the domestic role, as submissive, which results in a lack of positive role models for women; â€¢ eating disorders â€“ the media has been criticised for the â€˜beauty mythâ€™ and its use of image manipulation that create impossible ideal body types for both males and females to live up to; â€¢ media violence â€“ many media effects theories argue that the media glamourises violence and thus is responsible for violence in society (hypodermic needle, BOBO doll); â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 26 of 31 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Describe two limitations of official crime statistics. Possible answers: â€¢ dark figure of crime â€“ sociological research demonstrates that there is a large figure of crime that is not officially known about and so does not feature in the OCS making this measure incomplete; â€¢ unreported crime â€“ crimes not reported to the police will not feature in the OCS (e.g. a private affair, too embarrassed, afraid of repercussions etc . ) making them inaccurate; â€¢ unrecorded crime â€“ the police do not record all crimes reported to them and so these crimes do not appear in the OCS e.g. crime categorised as a family matter making them inaccurate; â€¢ victimless crimes â€“ crimes without an obvious victim will not feature in the OCS e.g. drug taking, status crimes making them inaccurate; â€¢ white - collar crime â€“ non - official measures of crime show that this type of crime is frequently not reported or recorded and so rarely appears in the OCS (Marxism) making them incomplete; â€¢ police discretion â€“ police forces and officers often focus on different crimes to one another meaning that reliability may be low, e.g. one force cracks down on drug dealing and targets and prosecutes offenders whilst another targets and prosecutes shoplif ting â€“ there is thus little comparability; â€¢ institutional racism â€“ stop and searches done by the police disproportionately feature ethnic minorities so the OCS are biased and ethnic minorities are thus over - represented in the OCS; â€¢ quantitative data â€“ statistics are only numbers and therefore do not show reasons for crimes being committed or the impact upon victims they lack validity; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4 0495 / 22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 20 of 32 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Describe two measurements of crime used by sociologists. Possible answers: â€¢ official statistics â€“ crimes recorded by the police and processed through the criminal justice system; â€¢ court records â€“ total number of convictions for different offences and records of the social characteristics of offenders; â€¢ self - report studies â€“ research that asks people what crimes or deviant acts they have committed; â€¢ victim surveys â€“ research that asks people what crimes they have been victims of e.g. Crime Survey of England and Wales; â€¢ local victim surveys â€“ e.g. Islington survey â€“ asking people what crimes they have been victims of but focusing only on a specified local area to understand the impact of crime there; â€¢ feminist victim surveys â€“ e.g. Walklateâ€™s research â€“ asking women about what crimes they have been victims of, looking specifically at domestic and sexual crimes and the fear of crime, using a qualitative methodology; â€¢ any other reasonable response. One mark for each point correctly identified (up to a maximum of two ). One mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). 4

3(c) Explain how stereotyping can affect policing. subject: Sociology
question_text: Research Methods, Identity and Inequality You must answer on the enclosed answer booklet. You will need: Answer booklet (enclosed) INSTRUCTIONS â€¢ Answer two questions in total: Answer Question 1. Answer either Question 2 or Question 3. â€¢ Follow the instructions on the front cover of the answer booklet. If you need additional answer paper, ask the invigilator for a continuation booklet. INFORMATION â€¢ The total mark for this paper is 80. â€¢ The number of marks for each question or part question is shown in brackets [ ]. This document has 8 pages. Any blank pages are indicated. [Turn over 06_0495_11_2025_1.13 *6067250012* Answer Question 1. Research methods Read Source A and then answer the questions below. Source A In the United States Barbara Ehrenreich used covert participant observation to research how working class people are able to live on low wages. For several months Ehrenreich left her middle-class home and lived and worked with people in low-wage jobs. She found work in four different cities in the United States. Her various jobs included being a waitress, a cleaner, a care home assistant and a shop worker. She wrote down her experiences at the end of each day. For her research Ehrenreich experienced trying to live on a low wage to pay for her food, clothing, transportation and shelter. Using covert participant observation allowed Ehrenreich to gain a first-hand, qualitative and in-depth understanding of the difficult lives of low-wage workers. Her conclusions were: â€¢ low-wage workers cannot afford basic necessities without working in more than one job â€¢ low-wage workers are often socially controlled through random searches of their bags and drug testing â€¢ managers had negative attitudes to low-wage workers based on class or racial prejudice â€¢ medical treatment and childcare are too expensive for low-wage workers. Source: adapted from an internet summary of â€˜Nickel and Dimedâ€™ by Barbara Ehrenreich. (a) From Source A , identify two conclusions of Ehrenreich`s research. [2] (i) Using information in Source A , explain two reasons why the research is useful for studying the experiences of low-wage workers. [4] (ii) Using information in Source A , explain three problems with the research for understanding the experiences of low-wage workers. [6] (iii) State two aspects of a positivist approach to sociological research. [2] (b) Explain one strength and one limitation of semi-structured interviews. [4] (c) Evaluate the macro approach to sociological research. Your answer should include: (d) â€¢ at least two arguments for and two arguments against â€¢ a conclusion. [10] 06_0495_11_2025_1.13
mark_scheme_text:
1(a)(i) From Source A, identify two conclusions of Ehrenreichâ€™s research. Award one mark for each point correctly identified from Source A (up to a maximum of two ). Possible answers: â€¢ workers cannot afford basic necessities without working in more than one job; â€¢ in low - wage jobs workers are socially controlled through random searches of their bags and drug testing; â€¢ medical treatment and childcare are too expensive for low - wage workers â€¢ managers had negative attitudes to low - wage workers based on class or racial prejudice. 2

1(a)(ii) Using information in Source A, explain two reasons why the research is useful for studying the experiences of low - wage workers. Award one mark for each point correctly identified from Source A (up to a maximum of two ). Award one mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of two ). Possible answers: â€¢ it was a participant observation â€“ she worked as a waitress, a cleaner, a nursing home assistant, and a shop worker, which gave her an insiderâ€™s view of what it feels like to be in a low - wage job; â€¢ it was a covert observation â€“ being undercover would allow her to gain an honest picture of the lives of those on low wages; â€¢ Ehrenreich experienced trying to live on a low wage to pay for her food, clothing, transportation and shelter â€“ this means that she would experience first - hand the difficulties of making her wages cover her living costs, adding to validity; â€¢ the data was collected over several months â€“ it thus offers more than a snapshot and allowed Ehrenreich to gain a lot of detailed data; â€¢ she uses the method of observation â€“ this method allows Ehrenreich to see and witness first - hand the daily grind of the jobs and how difficult it is to exist on a minimum wage this gives the data good validity; â€¢ the data was gathered from four different cities â€“ this means that it is more representative than research based solely in one place; â€¢ it is a first - hand, qualitative and in - depth understanding of the difficult lives of low - wage workers â€“ this allowed Ehrenreich to gain a full understanding of workersâ€™ lives (verstehen); â€¢ any other reasonable answer. 4 0495 / 11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 15 of 30 Question Answer Marks

1(a)(iii) Using information in Source A, explain three problems with the research for understanding the experiences of low - wage workers. Award one mark for each point correctly identified from Source A (up to a maximum of three ). Award one mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of three ). Possible answers: â€¢ data was written down at end of day â€“ the researcher will likely have been tired and forgotten to include some events, insights, negatively affecting validity of her findings about low wage work; â€¢ the research depended on Ehrenreich who is only one researcher â€“ researcher subjectivity may mean that her interpretation of events that occurred may have been differ
