[Turn over 06_0495_13_2025_1.13 *2858088951* Answer Question 1. Research methods Read Source A and then answer the questions below. Source A The British Social Attitudes (BSA) survey studies public opinion on social and political issues in Britain. The survey has been done each year since 1983. Researchers interview participants in 1 their homes using a structured interview with 300 closed pre-coded questions. The questions in the survey ask about issues such as welfare benefits, health care, education, transport, the environment, immigration and crime. Participants are selected for the survey using random sampling. The population of Britain is approximately 67 million people. The sample for the survey is 4000 adults who live in Britain. A key aim of the BSA survey is to study changes in public opinion on social and political issues over time, so some questions are the same and repeated every year. Other questions in the survey are different every year. The BSA survey collects quantitative data and produces statistics. These statistics make the survey useful for both government policy making and sociologists doing research. These gendered role models are seen by pupils and hence maintain expec tations and gender inequalities; â€¢ access to education â€“ globally girls are less likely to attend school than boys as males are more highly valued and seen as future breadwinners in many cultures. This maintains gender inequalities in society; â€¢ gendered curriculum â€“ feminists say the school curriculum offers a male - centred view of the world e.g. through the male authors studied in literature to images in textbooks of boys doing more interesting and active things to the focus on male achievement and experiences in his tory â€“ this all helps to maintain gender inequalities; â€¢ subject choice â€“ some subjects are still only for girls (e.g. needlework) or only for boys (e.g. woodwork) and even in countries where this isn't the case, boys and girls still choose different subjects which affect life chances; â€¢ peer groups â€“ peer groups can influence attitudes towards education and the peer group may apply pressure to conform to gender expectations e.g. arranged marriages in some cultures â€“ this can help to maintain gender inequalities; â€¢ teacher expectations â€“ these do vary culturally but teachers tend to have lower expectations of girls and are

2(a)(i) Define the term linguistic factors. Award one mark for a partial definition, e.g. language . Award two marks for a clear definition, e.g. the type of language children speak e.g. restricted and elaborated codes. 2

2(a)(ii) Define the term conformity. Award one mark for a partial definition, e.g. fitting in . Award two marks for a clear definition, e.g. behaviour that follows the usual standards and expectations of a group, culture and/or society. 2

2(b) Give two examples of an ethnocentric curriculum. Award one mark for each correct example (up to a maximum of two ). Possible responses: â€¢ host nation â€“ their norms, values and beliefs are embedded into the educational curriculum; â€¢ language teaching â€“ the language and cultures of ethnic minority learners may be seen by the school as being of lesser value; â€¢ teaching of literature â€“ poetry and books from the culture of ethnic minority learners may not be considered worth studying; â€¢ teaching of history â€“ learners from ethnic minority groups may learn nothing about their own peopleâ€™s experiences; â€¢ religion and worship in assemblies â€“ the ethnic minority learnersâ€™ beliefs may be ignored and they may even be forced to follow the practices of the majority; â€¢ celebration of festivals based on the dominant culture â€“ ethnic minority learnersâ€™ festivals may be ignored; â€¢ any other reasonable response. 2 0495 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 16 of 26 Question Answer Marks

2(c) Describe three benefits of attending private school. Award one mark for each point correctly identified (up to a maximum of three ). Award one mark for each point that is developed (up to a maximum of three ). Possible responses: â€¢ class sizes â€“ classes in private schools tend to be smaller, so individual learners get more attention from the teacher; â€¢ more choice in curriculum â€“ private schools do not have to follow a national curriculum so they can concentrate on teaching what they believe to be most important and/or what their parents want; â€¢ parental choice â€“ private schools are very diverse institutions and so parents can choose the school that best suits their childrenâ€™s needs; â€¢ money and funding â€“ private schools have more money than state schools meaning they have better resources and facilities for the students e.g. swimming pools, ICT, science equipment etc . , often also resulting in better results; â€¢ social capital â€“ Bourdieu â€“ private schools allow students to make networks and connections that can help them find employment in high status professions and companies in the future; â€¢ extra - curricular opportunities â€“ private schools are renowned for the plethora of extra - curricular activities on offer, allowing students to experience a wide variety of sports, culture, arts, theatre etc . and so increasing cultural capital (Bourdieu); â€¢ life chances and social mobility â€“ private schools typically gain better examination res
